name: al-'Ababsah
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal lineage and pastoral territorial rights
ancestorStory: Named after their ancestor Abbas, part of the broader tribal confederations of eastern Arabia. The tribe traces its lineage through traditional Bedouin genealogical systems.
description: Al-'Ababsah is a tribal branch historically present in eastern Arabia, particularly in areas that are now part of Saudi Arabia and the UAE. They were traditionally nomadic pastoralists who moved between seasonal grazing grounds in the Arabian Peninsula. The tribe maintained traditional Bedouin lifestyle and social structures, with some sections settling in oasis communities. Today, members of the tribe are integrated into modern Gulf societies while maintaining their tribal identity.

relations.context: Both tribes shared overlapping migration routes in eastern Arabia
Historical cooperation in eastern Arabian territories

name: Abd al-Qays
legitimacyNotes: Ancient Arab tribe with established genealogical records and historical presence in eastern Arabia
ancestorStory: Named after their ancestor Abd al-Qays ibn Afsa ibn Adi ibn Haritha ibn Amr ibn Amir. The tribe traces its lineage to the ancient Rabi'ah branch of Adnan.
description: Abd al-Qays was a prominent pre-Islamic and early Islamic Arab tribe based in eastern Arabia, particularly in the region of Bahrain (historical Bahrain province) and parts of modern-day eastern Saudi Arabia. They were among the first tribes to embrace Islam during Prophet Muhammad's lifetime, sending a delegation to Medina. The tribe was known for their involvement in maritime trade across the Arabian Gulf and their agricultural settlements in the oasis regions. While historically significant, they largely merged into other tribal formations over the centuries.

relations.context: Both tribes have historical connections to Bahrain region
Both involved in early Arabian Gulf maritime activities

name: Abdah
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal authority based on genealogical descent from Shammar confederation
ancestorStory: Abdah is traced as a descendant of the greater Shammar tribal confederation. The branch established itself as a distinct lineage within the broader Shammar genealogical structure.
description: Abdah Shammar is a tribal branch of the larger Shammar confederation, one of the major Arabian tribal groups with Adnani lineage. Historically based in northern Arabia and Najd regions, they practiced nomadic pastoralism and participated in the complex tribal politics of central Arabia. As part of the broader Shammar network, they maintained traditional Bedouin lifestyle and social structures. The tribe remains active with members present across the Gulf region, particularly in Saudi Arabia and other GCC states.

relations.context: Historical competition for influence in Najd region between Shammar confederation and Saudi state

name: Abida
legitimacyNotes: Ancient Qahtani lineage with established tribal territory and recognized genealogy
ancestorStory: Descended from Abida ibn Muhalhal, a prominent figure in early Arabian tribal genealogy. The tribe traces its origins to the ancient Qahtani confederation.
description: Abida is an ancient Arabian tribe of Qahtani origin that has been present in the Arabian Peninsula since pre-Islamic times. They are traditionally based in the central and southern regions of the Arabian Peninsula, with some branches extending into modern-day Saudi Arabia. The tribe played a role in early Islamic conquests and maintained their tribal structure through various historical periods. Today, members of Abida can be found across the GCC states, though they maintain stronger presence in Saudi Arabia.

relations.context: Both tribes trace descent to Qahtani origins

name: Abookara
description: Abookara is a tribal clan or family group in the Arabian Peninsula region. Limited historical documentation is available regarding their specific origins, territorial base, or role in Gulf state formation. Their current status and activities within the GCC region require further genealogical research to establish their lineage connections and historical significance.

name: Abu Karaki
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal leadership through hereditary sheikhs
ancestorStory: Descended from Karak, an ancestor whose lineage traces to the broader tribal confederations of the Arabian Peninsula. The name reflects their connection to this founding figure.
description: Abu Karaki is a tribal branch found in parts of the Arabian Peninsula, primarily engaged in traditional pastoral activities. They maintain their tribal structure and identity within the broader social fabric of the region. Like many smaller tribal groups, they have adapted to modern conditions while preserving their cultural heritage. Their current influence and exact historical significance in Gulf affairs remains limited in documented sources.

name: Adnan (Adnanite lineage)
legitimacyNotes: Genealogical authority as progenitor of northern Arabian tribes
ancestorStory: Descended from Ishmael, son of Abraham
description: Northern Arabs / Arabized Arabs / al-Arab al-Mustarabah. Origin: Hejaz, Central/North Arabia.

subTribes.name: Mudar
subTribes.legitimacyNotes: Modern scholarship treats these genealogies with caution, noting that they reflect later attempts to systematize tribal relations and political rivalries rather than straightforward history. The confederation included diverse tribes with varying lifestyles and political orientations grouped together under the collective name 'Mudar'.
subTribes.ancestorName: Mudar b. Nizār b. Maʿadd b. ʿAdnān
subTribes.ancestorStory: Genealogists trace Mudar's descent from Mudar b. Nizār b. Maʿadd b. ʿAdnān, placing him among the foundational ancestors of the so-called 'northern Arabs'. Mudar is said to have had two sons, from whom the major branches of the confederation descended. Genealogical narratives also preserve legendary elements, such as the division of Nizār's inheritance and symbolic associations.
subTribes.traditionalEconomy: Mixed - ranging from sedentary groups associated with sanctuary towns to highly mobile pastoral nomads
subTribes.description: Mudar was one of the principal tribal confederations of northern and central Arabia in late antiquity and the early Islamic period. Rising to power over the Hejaz in the fifth century after supplanting the Banu Ghassan, Mudar occupied large areas of the Hejaz, Tihamah, Najd, and adjoining regions. Together with Rabīʿa, it formed one of the two largest groupings of tribes tracing descent from Nizar ibn Maʿadd. In later historiography, 'Mudar' came to designate not only a genealogical lineage but also a broad political and cultural bloc opposed to the southern (Yemeni) tribal groupings.

subTribes.name: Rabi'ah
subTribes.legitimacyNotes: Established blood lineage recognized by classical Arab genealogists as one of the two main Adnanite branches
subTribes.ancestorStory: Rabi'a ibn Nizar is considered the patriarch of one of the two main branches of the North Arabian (Adnanite) tribes, representing a major division in Arabian tribal genealogy.
subTribes.traditionalEconomy: Mixed - bedouin pastoralism and sedentary agriculture, with some branches being mostly sedentary while others remained nomadic
subTribes.description: Rabi'a ibn Nizar is the patriarch of one of two main branches of the 'North Arabian' (Adnanite) tribes, the other being founded by Mudhar. The tribe has numerous important branches including Abd al-Qays, Anizah, Banu Bakr, Taghlib, and others. Originally from the Tihamah region of western Arabia, they migrated northwards and eastwards, with different branches settling across the Arabian Peninsula, Mesopotamia, and eventually Egypt. Many were Christian before Islam, and the tribe produced numerous notable figures throughout Islamic history.

subTribes.name: Qays Aylan
subTribes.description: Key Adnanite branch

subTribes.name: Anizzah
subTribes.legitimacyNotes: Well-documented blood lineage within the established Rabiʿa-Adnanite genealogical framework, with historical continuity from pre-Islamic times through modern era.
subTribes.ancestorStory: The classical Arab genealogists placed Anizah within the large Rabiʿa branch of the Adnanites alongside tribes of Abd al-Qays, Banu Bakr, Bani Hanifa, and Taghlib. Anizah's eponymous ancestor is described as a great uncle of all these related tribes, indicating they come from the same family and most likely one patriarch.
subTribes.traditionalEconomy: Mixed economy of pastoral nomadism and tribute collection. Nomadic branches practiced seasonal migration between northern Arabian steppes and Syria, while levying crops from oasis inhabitants and collecting khuwwa (brotherhood tribute) from Syrian farmers in exchange for protection.
subTribes.description: Anizah (Arabic: عنزة) is a major Arabian tribe with presence across the Arabian Peninsula, Upper Mesopotamia, and the Levant. Their existence as an autonomous tribal group predates Islam, and they are classified within the Rabiʿa branch of the Adnanites. The tribe has both nomadic and sedentary branches, with the nomadic branch living in northern Arabian steppes and the sedentary branch in Al-Yamama district of eastern Najd. They became prominent masters of oasis towns like Khaybar and Al-'Ula during the Ottoman era, collecting tribute from agricultural communities while maintaining seasonal migration patterns.

subTribes.name: Bani Tamim
subTribes.legitimacyNotes: Claims direct Adnanite lineage through a well-documented genealogical tradition tracing back to Abraham through Ishmael. This appears to be a genuine blood lineage claim rather than a political confederation.
subTribes.ancestorStory: According to the traditional family tree, Tamim ibn Murr ibn 'Udd ibn Amr ibn Ilyas ibn Mudar bin Nizar bin Ma'add bin Adnan - claimed to be a direct descendant of Isma'il bin Ibrahim (Ishmael, son of Abraham).
subTribes.peakPowerEra: Early Islamic period through Abbasid era
subTribes.description: The Banū Tamīm are one of the largest Arab tribes that originated in Najd and Hejaz in the Arabian Peninsula. They are an Adnanite tribe with a claimed direct lineage to Abraham through Ishmael. The tribe occupied numerous Wadis and villages in central and eastern Arabia in the 6th century and played an important role in the beginning of Islam. They came into contact with Muhammad in the 8th year of Hijrah but did not immediately convert to Islam. The word Tamim in Arabic means strong and solid, and can also mean those who strive for perfection.

subTribes.name: Bani Yas
subTribes.legitimacyNotes: This is a genuine tribal confederation that formed through political alliance and shared settlement patterns, with the Al Bu Falah clan holding the most power. The confederation structure allowed for integration of associated tribes under Abu Dhabi's authority while maintaining some autonomous characteristics.
subTribes.ancestorStory: The Bani Yas tribe originated in the Najd region of Central Arabia, descending, along with the Awamir, from Yas ibn Amer ibn Sa'sa'a.
subTribes.description: The Bani Yas is a tribal confederation of Najdi origin in the United Arab Emirates, consisting of around 20 tribes from Abu Dhabi to Dubai to Khor Al Adaid in southeast Qatar. As the largest single tribe among the indigenous tribal population of what is now the UAE, they formed a powerful confederation called the Bani Yas Coalition. The Al Nahyan (Al Bu Falah branch) leads the tribe and rules Abu Dhabi, while the Al Maktoum (Al Bu Falasah branch) rules Dubai, making them the most influential families in UAE federal government.

subTribes.name: Otaibah
subTribes.legitimacyNotes: The tribe claims descent from pre-Islamic Hawazin through documented genealogical records. While most genealogy is based on oral tradition dating to the Middle Ages and earlier, there is scholarly verification through historical manuscripts including Hisham ibn al-Kalbi's 8th-century work 'The Great Ancestry'.
subTribes.ancestorName: Otaibah ibn Guzayah ibn Jusham ibn Sa'd ibn Bakr ibn Hawazin
subTribes.ancestorStory: According to the 8th-century manuscript by Hisham ibn al-Kalbi, 'Otaibah' is attributed to a standard or banner belonging to the tribe of Hawazin. The name derives from Otaibah Ibn Guzayah Ibn Jusham Ibn Sa'd ibn Bakr Ibn Hawazin. After most Hawazin clans departed from Hejaz and Najd, those who remained formed the largest Hawazin alliance and were named Otaibah.
subTribes.description: The Otaibah is one of the biggest Arabian tribes originating in the Arabian Peninsula, with distribution spanning throughout Saudi Arabia, especially in Najd and Hejaz, and the Middle East. They are descended from the Bedouin and trace back to the Mudar family, belonging to the Qays ʿAylān confederacy through its previous name, Hawazin. The tribe remained as the largest Hawazin alliance after most other Hawazin clans departed from Hejaz and Najd to other lands including the Levant, Iraq, Egypt, North Africa, and Persia.

subTribes.name: Harb
subTribes.legitimacyNotes: The text only briefly mentions 'Harb tribe in the Arabian peninsula' without providing details about its formation, lineage claims, or historical context.
subTribes.ancestorStory: <UNKNOWN>
subTribes.description: Harb is an Arabian Peninsula tribe mentioned in the research text, though specific details about the tribe itself are not provided in the source material.

subTribes.name: Mutayr
subTribes.legitimacyNotes: The tribal lineage appears to be authentic blood lineage supported by DNA testing showing shared haplogroups and common ancestry among members. The tribe has embraced genetic genealogy to study their genealogy using modern technologies.
subTribes.ancestorStory: According to Ahmad al-Qalqashandi (d. 1418), the Mutayr tribe belongs to Ghatafan who are descendants of Ishmael son of Abraham. DNA tests have confirmed that male participants from Mutayr share the same haplogroup and common ancestor, with most Y-Chromosome lineage from the J1 Haplogroup network.
subTribes.description: Mutayr is a large Arab tribe originating from the northern Hejaz near Medina, whose territory extends from the Hijaz to Najd and then to Kuwait. The tribe underwent a major eastward migration in the 17th century into central Arabia, becoming the dominant nomadic tribe of Al-Qaseem. They played a crucial role in early 20th century Arabian history through their involvement in the Ikhwan movement under Faisal Al-Dewish. Originally Bedouin, the tribe has now largely settled in cities and towns across Saudi Arabia, establishing up to 400 villages across the country.

subTribes.name: Banu Khalid
subTribes.legitimacyNotes: Appears to be a genuine tribal lineage with established chieftainship structure through the Al Humaid clan
subTribes.traditionalEconomy: desert nomadism and control of trade routes
subTribes.description: The Bani Khalid were a powerful Arabian tribe that dominated eastern Arabia from the 17th to 19th centuries. They established the Bani Khalid Emirate in 1669 under Emir Barrak ibn Ghurayr, controlling the Al-Hasa and Al-Qatif regions after expelling Ottoman forces. The tribe's chieftainship was traditionally held by the Al Humaid clan. Known for their military prowess, they were staunch enemies of the emerging Saudi state and the Wahhabi movement. Their power declined after repeated defeats by Saudi-tribal alliances, with their rule ending definitively in 1830, though they briefly regained some authority under Ottoman patronage in 1874.

subTribes.name: Subay'
subTribes.legitimacyNotes: Established blood lineage tracing to the ancient and powerful Banu 'Amir tribe that dominated central Arabia in medieval times
subTribes.ancestorStory: The family traces its lineage to the large, ancient tribe of Banu 'Amir, also known as 'Amir ibn Sa'sa'a, who came to dominate central Arabia in the medieval period.
subTribes.description: Subaie' is an Arabian tribe living in the center of southern Najd. The tribe comprises both nomadic (bedouin) and sedentary sections, with the sedentary members having resided in central Arabia for centuries. Families belonging to Subay' can be found in practically every town in the region, making up a disproportionate amount of central Arabia's traditional sedentary population. They were known as Prophet Mohammed's knights and have served as knights for the first and second Saudi states as well as the current Kingdom of Saudi Arabia.

subTribes.name: Banu Hanifa
subTribes.legitimacyNotes: Classical Arab genealogists counted them as a Christian branch of Bani Bakr, but they led an independent existence prior to Islam. Later assimilated into Anizah tribe.
subTribes.description: Banu Hanifa is an ancient Arab tribe that inhabited the area of al-Yamama in central Saudi Arabia. Members of the great Rabi'ah branch of North Arabian tribes, they were primarily sedentary farmers living in settlements along the wadis of eastern Nejd. The tribe played a significant role in early Islamic history, initially converting to Islam but then apostatizing under their own prophet Musaylima, leading to the Ridda Wars. After their defeat, they rejoined the Islamic state and later spread to various regions including Iraq and Egypt. The ruling House of Saud of Saudi Arabia are Hanifites, and according to King Salman Al Saud, the tribe eventually assimilated into the larger Anizah tribe.

relations.context: Al Saud trace Adnanite descent
Qasimi lineage traces to Adnanite origins
Al Nahyan claim Adnanite descent

name: Adwan
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal authority based on Hawazin lineage and historical presence in Hejaz
ancestorName: Adwan ibn Amr ibn Qays Aylan
ancestorStory: Descended from Adwan ibn Amr, part of the Hawazin tribal confederation. The tribe traces its lineage through Qays Aylan to Adnan.
description: Adwan was a prominent pre-Islamic Arabian tribe belonging to the Hawazin confederation, traditionally based in the Hejaz region. They were known as one of the major Adnani tribes and played significant roles in pre-Islamic tribal politics and early Islamic conquests. The tribe was particularly active in the Najd and western Arabian Peninsula regions. While historically important, their influence diminished over time and they are now primarily of genealogical significance to modern Arabian tribal structures.

relations.context: Both trace Adnani origins through different branches

name: Afar
legitimacyNotes: recognized sub-section of the larger Awamir tribal confederation
description: The Afar are a sub-tribe of the larger Awamir confederation, part of the Adnani Arab lineage. They have historically been nomadic pastoralists moving across the borders of what is now the UAE, Oman, and Saudi Arabia. Like other Awamir sections, they have maintained their tribal identity while some members have settled in modern Gulf states. They remain an active part of the broader Awamir tribal structure in the contemporary period.

relations.context: both tribes traditionally migrated across similar desert territories in eastern Arabian Peninsula

name: Ahl al-Buwait
description: Ahl al-Buwait is a clan-level tribal group in the Arabian Peninsula. Limited historical documentation exists regarding their specific origins, territorial base, or major historical roles. Their current status and activities within the GCC region remain unclear from available historical sources.

name: Ahl al-Sumada
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal authority through lineage and consensus
description: Ahl al-Sumada is a tribal group mentioned in Arabian Peninsula genealogical records. Limited historical documentation makes it difficult to establish their specific territorial base, major historical roles, or current status within the GCC region. Further research would be needed to confirm their lineage connections and historical significance.

name: Ajman
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal leadership and historical control of territory in eastern Arabian Peninsula
ancestorStory: Descended from Ajman ibn Yasir of the Yam tribe. The confederation formed through alliance of related clans in the eastern Arabian Peninsula.
description: The Ajman are a major Bedouin tribal confederation historically based in eastern Saudi Arabia and the UAE. They were one of the most powerful nomadic tribes in the region during the 18th and 19th centuries, controlling vast territories and trade routes. The tribe played significant roles in regional conflicts and the formation of modern Gulf states. Today they remain influential with members holding citizenship across GCC countries, particularly in Saudi Arabia and the UAE.

Encroached on Bani Khalid territories during their decline and fought against them in 1823

Cooperation in Gulf maritime activities

name: Ajman (tribe)
legitimacyNotes: Established tribal confederation with clear lineage to Banu Yam and Qahtanite roots, though now mostly settled rather than nomadic
ancestorStory: Al-Ajman is a Qahtanite Arab tribe that is descended from Banu Yam tribe.
description: Al-Ajman or al-'Ijman is an Arabian tribal confederation in the Arabian Peninsula, with Ajman spread across Saudi Arabia, Qatar, the United Arab Emirates and Kuwait. The Ajman were noted for being important players in the politics of eastern Arabia in the 18th and early 20th centuries. Nearly all the Ajman have abandoned nomadic life and have settled in the Persian Gulf states, particularly in the Eastern Province of Saudi Arabia.

Cousin tribe sharing same Arabic dialect group

Allied with Ajman in Ikhwan rebellion against Ibn Saud

name: Al Bin Ali
legitimacyNotes: Established merchant family with historical ruling authority in Zubara and involvement in Gulf politics
ancestorStory: The Al Bin Ali trace their lineage to Ali bin Sulaiman, who established the family's presence in the Gulf region. They became prominent merchants and political figures in Qatar and Bahrain.
description: The Al Bin Ali are a prominent merchant family originally from Najd who settled in the Gulf region and became influential in Qatar and Bahrain. They established the town of Zubara in northern Qatar in the 18th century, which became a major pearling and trading center. The family played significant roles in Gulf politics and commerce, with branches in both Qatar and Bahrain. They remain active today with continued presence in business and government positions across the Gulf states.

relations.context: Historical conflicts over Bahrain and Zubara

name: Al Jalahma
legitimacyNotes: merchant family with pearling fleet ownership and trading networks
ancestorStory: Al Jalahma are believed to descend from Jalahma, who migrated from Najd to the Gulf coast. The family established themselves as prominent pearling merchants and sea captains.
description: Al Jalahma are a prominent merchant family originally from Najd who settled in the Gulf region. They became influential pearling merchants and sea captains, particularly active in Qatar and Bahrain waters. The family played significant roles in Gulf maritime trade networks and had notable interactions with other ruling families. They remain active in business and social circles across the GCC states today.

relations.context: historical conflicts over pearling territories and trade routes
cooperation in pearling and maritime activities

name: Al-Sabah
legitimacyNotes: Ruling family of Kuwait, political leadership through historical governance and state founding
ancestorStory: Sabah bin Jaber was chosen as the first ruler of Kuwait around 1752 by the merchant families who had settled in the area. The family established political control over Kuwait through consensus leadership among the trading community.
description: Al-Sabah is the ruling dynasty of Kuwait, tracing their political authority to 1752 when Sabah bin Jaber was chosen as leader by Kuwait's merchant community. Originally from the Anizah tribal confederation, they migrated from Najd to the Kuwait area in the early 18th century. The family has maintained continuous rule over Kuwait for over 270 years, surviving Ottoman influence, British protectorate status, Iraqi invasion, and establishing modern Kuwait as an independent state in 1961. They remain the constitutional monarchy ruling Kuwait today.

relations.context: Both dynasties migrated from Najd to the Gulf coast in the 18th century and established ruling houses

name: Al Abdul Dayem
description: Al Abdul Dayem is a tribal grouping whose specific historical details and geographical distribution within the Arabian Peninsula require further documentation. Without verified historical sources, their exact role in Gulf regional politics and tribal networks remains unclear. Further genealogical research would be needed to establish their lineage connections and historical significance in GCC context.

name: Al'ahmad
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal leadership through patrilineal descent
ancestorStory: Named after their progenitor Ahmad, though specific genealogical details vary among different branches.
description: Al Ahmad is a widespread clan name found across various tribal groups in the Arabian Peninsula and Gulf region. Multiple unrelated tribal branches carry this patronymic, making it one of the more common clan designations. Different Al Ahmad groups have varying origins and historical backgrounds, with some integrated into larger tribal confederations throughout the GCC states. Their current status and specific roles vary significantly depending on their particular lineage and geographic location.

name: Al-'Aljanat
description: Al-'Aljanat is a tribal clan whose specific historical details and geographical origins within the Arabian Peninsula remain unclear in available sources. The name suggests a tribal grouping that may have been part of the broader tribal confederation system of the region. Limited documentation makes it difficult to establish their definitive role in Gulf state formation or their current status within the GCC tribal landscape.

name: Al Araytham
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal leadership through hereditary sheikhs
description: Al Araytham is a small tribal group found in the Arabian Peninsula. They maintain traditional tribal structure with limited documented historical prominence. Current status and specific regional distribution within the GCC states requires further documentation. Their role in modern Gulf society appears to be primarily at the local community level.

name: Al'asead
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal authority through ancestral lineage and historical presence in Arabian Peninsula
ancestorStory: Al Asead traces their lineage to an ancestor named Asead, though specific genealogical details vary among different branches of the family.
description: Al Asead is a tribal grouping found across various regions of the Arabian Peninsula, with branches present in different Gulf states. Like many Arabian tribes, they have historically been involved in traditional Bedouin activities including pastoralism and trade. The tribe maintains its tribal identity and social structures in modern times, though specific historical records about their major political or military activities are limited. Various branches of Al Asead have integrated into the social fabric of contemporary Gulf societies.

name: Al-Askar
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal authority based on lineage and historical presence in the region
description: Al-Askar is a tribal group found in various parts of the Arabian Peninsula. The name 'Askar' relates to military or soldier origins, suggesting possible historical connections to military service or warrior traditions. Limited detailed historical records make it difficult to trace their specific origins and migrations within the Gulf region.

name: Al-Aslam
legitimacyNotes: Branch of the major Shammar confederation with traditional grazing rights
ancestorStory: Al-Aslam traces its lineage to Aslam, a prominent ancestor within the broader Shammar tribal structure. They established themselves as a distinct branch while maintaining ties to the greater Shammar confederation.
description: Al-Aslam is a tribal branch of the larger Shammar confederation, one of the most prominent tribal groups in the Arabian Peninsula. Historically based in the northern Arabian Peninsula, they participated in the traditional nomadic pastoralist economy and seasonal migrations. As part of Shammar, they were involved in the complex tribal politics of the region during the rise of the Saudi state. Today, members of Al-Aslam are found across the Gulf states, particularly in Saudi Arabia and Kuwait, where they maintain their tribal identity while adapting to modern sedentary life.

relations.context: Both are branches of the greater Shammar confederation
Historical alliances in northern Arabian Peninsula tribal conflicts

name: Al Aunan
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal authority through lineage and alliance networks
description: Al Aunan is a tribal group with historical presence in the Arabian Peninsula. They maintain traditional alliance networks with other Gulf tribes, notably Al Bu Falah. Their current status and specific regional influence within the GCC states remains documented through their ongoing tribal connections and social structures.

relations.context: Allied during seasonal migrations and nomadic movements

name: Al-Awazim
legitimacyNotes: Claims direct descent from Adnani lineage through a detailed genealogical chain tracing back to Prophet Ishmael
ancestorStory: According to historical documents, they descend from Azem bin Hind bin Hilal bin Nufayl bin Rabiah bin Kilab bin Rabiah bin Amer bin Sa`sa bin Muawiyah bin Bakr bin Hawazin bin Mansour bin Ikrimah bin Khasfa bin Qais Aylan bin Mudar bin Nizar bin Ma`d bin Adnan, which goes back to the Islamic Prophet Ishmael, son of Abraham.
traditionalEconomy: nomadic pastoralism and later urban settlement
description: Al-Awazem is a large tribal confederation in the Arabian Peninsula, Syria, Lebanon, Jordan and Egypt, with the majority of its current settled population residing in Kuwait, northeastern Saudi Arabia, and Bahrain. They are among the largest tribes in Kuwait and were historically known for their military prowess, having fought the Ottomans, Achaemenids (Iranians), and Bani Rasheed. They once fought seven tribes to protect the Emirate of Diriyah.

Al Awazem historically had very strong alliances with Al Kathiri in most of the wars

name: Al-Azaznah tribe
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal authority based on lineage and historical presence in eastern Arabian Peninsula
description: Al-Azaznah is a tribal group historically present in the eastern Arabian Peninsula region. Limited historical documentation exists about this particular tribal branch, which appears to have been part of the broader tribal confederation system in the Gulf region. Their historical presence is noted in some genealogical records but detailed accounts of their activities and territorial control remain scarce. Current status and modern presence require further verification through tribal genealogical sources.

name: Al-Bahri
legitimacyNotes: maritime expertise and coastal territorial control
description: Al-Bahri is a maritime tribal group historically associated with coastal areas of the Arabian Gulf. The name 'Bahri' derives from 'bahr' meaning sea, indicating their traditional connection to maritime activities. They were historically involved in pearling, fishing, and coastal trade across various Gulf emirates. The tribe maintains presence in multiple Gulf states today, though with diminished traditional maritime focus.

relations.context: historical cooperation in Gulf maritime activities
coastal settlement patterns in UAE region

name: Al Bu Falah
legitimacyNotes: Originally from Bani Malik lineage but transformed into a confederation through warfare alliance against Ottoman influence. Various sections claim different Arab tribal origins but united under Albu Salih identity.
traditionalEconomy: agriculture in marshland areas, pastoral nomadism, and trade
description: The Albu Salih is an Arab tribe from the Bani Malik of the Al-Muntafiq confederation. During the 18th century, they transformed from a traditional tribe into a powerful tribal confederation (warfare alliance) in opposition to Ottoman Empire influence. Under the leadership of Shaikh Badr Al Rumaiyadh, they became the most influential tribe in the Bani Malik region of the Muntafiq Emirate. The tribe historically consisted of both settled villagers near the great marshes and semi-nomadic Bedouins who migrated seasonally between their territory in southern Iraq and the Arabian Gulf coast. They were known to be well-armed and engaged in trade during their migrations. By the 1940s-1950s, most nomadic members had settled permanently.

description: The Albuflasa (Al Bu Falasah) is described as a Bedouin clan with members in Bahrain. The tribe appears to be integrated into modern Bahraini society, with members serving in various professional capacities including the military and civil service.

name: Al Bu Hamir
legitimacyNotes: A genuine subsection of the larger Bani Yas confederation, historically recognized as part of the Manasir tribal structure
ancestorStory: <UNKNOWN>
description: The Al Bu Hamir is a small section of the Bani Yas tribal confederation, originating in Al Ain Region of the emirate of Abu Dhabi. They were considered a subsection of the Manasir under the Bani Yas structure. The tribe comprised approximately 60 houses in Abu Dhabi around the turn of the 20th century, where they appear to have settled. The tribe has both nomadic and sedentary branches, with the nomadic branch living in northern Arabian steppes and the sedentary branch in Al-Yamama district of eastern Najd. They became prominent masters of oasis towns like Khaybar and Al-'Ula during the Ottoman era, collecting tribute from agricultural communities while maintaining seasonal migration patterns.

The confederation includes prominent ruling families like Al-Sabah and Al-Khalifa, and successfully conquered Bahrain in 1783, establishing lasting rule there.

description: The Aws and Khazraj were two major Arab tribes that dominated Medina (Yathrib) before and during the early Islamic period. Originally from Yemen as part of the Azd confederation, they migrated north and established themselves as the primary inhabitants of the Medina oasis. They are historically significant as the Ansar (supporters) who welcomed Prophet Muhammad and the early Muslim community to Medina in 622 CE. While not directly part of the modern GCC region, their Yemeni Azd origins connect them to tribal lineages that later spread throughout the Arabian Peninsula, including those in current Gulf states.

name: Azd
legitimacyNotes: Ancient Arabian tribe with documented pre-Islamic inscriptions from 210-230 CE confirming their territorial presence. Genetic evidence suggests J-BY74 haplogroup associated with Azdite tribes originated in Northern Arabia or the Levant.

traditionalEconomy: trade and conquest - chief merchant group of Oman and Al-Ubulla, organized trading diaspora with settlements on coasts of Kirman and Makran
description: The Azd (Arabic: أَزْد), or Al-Azd (Arabic: ٱلْأَزْد), is an ancient Arabian tribe originating from Northern Arabia. The lands of Azd occupied an area west of Bisha and Al Bahah in what is today Saudi Arabia. The Azd tribal group originally lived in Yemen according to medieval folklore, until the collapse of the Ma'rib Dam in the late 3rd century CE when they began emigrating to other parts of the Arabian Peninsula.

subTribes.name: Zahran
subTribes.legitimacyNotes: This appears to be a genuine blood lineage tracing back to the ancient Azd confederation, with well-documented genealogical records and historical presence in the Arabian Peninsula.

subTribes.ancestorStory: Zahran is the name of the shared common ancestor of the tribe. Etymological sources indicate it is of Arabic Semitic origin, meaning 'bright' and 'pure'. The full lineage traces back to Al-Azd through Zahran ibn Ka'ab.
subTribes.description: Zahran (Arabic: زهران), also known as Banū ʿZahrān ibn Kaʿab, is one of the oldest Arabian tribes in the Arabian Peninsula and is regarded as one of the largest tribes in Al Bahah Province. Al Baha is the homeland of Zahran and Ghamid. The tribe is well-known from pre-Islamic times and many joined Prophet Muhammad in Medina during the early Islamic period. The name Zahran means 'bright' and 'pure' in Arabic.

subTribes.name: Ghamid
subTribes.legitimacyNotes: Documented blood lineage tracing back through Azd Shanu'ah to Qahtan, with clear genealogical records and tribal presence maintained from pre-Islamic times to present
subTribes.ancestorStory: The Ghamid tribe traces its lineage back to their forefather Ghamid, who is Amr ibn Ka'b — and some say Amr ibn Abdullah ibn Ka'b — ibn Al-Harith ibn Ka'b ibn Abdullah ibn Malik ibn Nasr, who is Shanu'ah of the Azd. There are different etymological explanations for the name, with Abu Hatim al-Sijistani and al-Asma'i stating it derives from 'Ghamadtu al-bi'r Ghamdan' meaning abundant water.
subTribes.traditionalEconomy: Agriculture and trade (benefited from Arabia Felix region with more rainfall and productive fields)
subTribes.description: The Ghamd (also transliterated as Ghamid) is an Azd Arab tribe of the Hejaz Region, predominantly Sunni, and considered one of the oldest tribes of the Arabian Peninsula. The Ghamid people are thought to be closely related to the neighboring tribe of Zahran. They have maintained their presence in their historical homeland from pre-Islamic times to the present day, and were significantly involved in early Islamic expansion and various historical conflicts.

During the early Islamic period, they participated in the Ridda wars and subsequent Arab conquests. Many of their descendants merged into other tribal formations or migrated, though their genealogical influence remains traced in various Gulf tribal lineages.

They are an agrarian mountain people who cultivated extensive farmland in the wadis of the Hajar Mountains, particularly Wadi Naqab and Wadi Bih. The tribe is known for speaking the distinctive Shehhi dialect of Arabic with Himyarite Yemeni origins dating to the second century BCE. Fiercely independent, they successfully resisted taxation attempts and maintained semi-autonomous status well into the 1960s. Their name is derived from their original holdings on Jebel Hibs mountain.

They migrated to Lower Najd in the early 13th century under Sheikh Mohammed Bin Shaba'an, then later to Al Ahsa'a. They are considered nomadic Bedouins, though many have settled in cities and hold government offices. They were honored by King Abdulaziz Al Saud with the title 'Khaznat Thafar' (Vault of Victory) for their crucial role in the Battle of Kinzan against Al Ajman in 1914-1915.

At the turn of the 19th century, the tribe comprised some 2,100 nomadic Bedouin (600 fighting men) and 2,700 settled people. They were highly influential in the political affairs of the interior Trucial States, with their territory stretching from south of Buraimi oasis to the eastern foothills of Hajar Mountains. The Northern branch settled in Dhaid and Al Falayah, while the Southern Bani Qitab (500 households) settled around Aflaj Bani Qitab in the Dhahirah area.

The word Tamim in Arabic means strong and solid, and can also mean those who strive for perfection.

description: The Bani Yas is a tribal confederation of Najdi origin in the United Arab Emirates, consisting of around 20 tribes from Abu Dhabi to Dubai to Khor Al Adaid in southeast Qatar. As the largest single tribe among the indigenous tribal population of what is now the UAE, they formed a powerful confederation called the Bani Yas Coalition. The Al Nahyan (Al Bu Falah branch) leads the tribe and rules Abu Dhabi, while the Al Maktoum (Al Bu Falasah branch) rules Dubai, making them the most influential families in UAE federal government.

subTribes.name: Mazrui
subTribes.description: The Mazrui or Mazar'i were an Omani Arab clan that reigned over some areas of East Africa, especially Kenya, from the 18th to the 20th century. In the 18th century they governed Mombasa and other coastal places and opposed the Omani Al Bu Sa'id Dynasty that ruled over Zanzibar. They were one of the groups that most actively resisted British rule when the British East Africa Protectorate was established in the late 19th century, along with the Kikuyu and Kamba people.

subTribes.description: The Albu Salih is an Arab tribe from the Bani Malik of the Al-Muntafiq confederation. During the 18th century, they transformed from a traditional tribe into a powerful tribal confederation (warfare alliance) in opposition to Ottoman Empire influence. Under the leadership of Shaikh Badr Al Rumaiyadh, they became the most influential tribe in the Bani Malik region of the Muntafiq Emirate. The tribe historically consisted of both settled villagers near the great marshes and semi-nomadic Bedouins who migrated seasonally between their territory in southern Iraq and the Arabian Gulf coast. They were known to be well-armed and engaged in trade during their migrations. By the 1940s-1950s, most nomadic members had settled permanently.

The tribe has been distributed across various Emirates including Dubai, Sharjah, Ajman, Umm Al Quwain, and Ras Al Khaimah.

The tribe was involved in Meccan trade networks and later played important roles in Islamic expansion. While historically significant in the Arabian Peninsula's tribal structure, they are not among the major tribal confederations currently active in the modern GCC states.

Various branches of Banu Al-Harith have settled in different regions of the Gulf, with some maintaining their traditional pastoral lifestyle while others have integrated into modern Gulf societies. The confederation remains active today with descendants found throughout the GCC states.

The confederation included numerous sub-tribes and clans that later dispersed throughout the Arabian Peninsula. The confederation played significant roles in pre-Islamic tribal warfare, including conflicts with Banu Taghlib and involvement in the famous Basus War. Descended from the Azd tribes of Yemen, they migrated north following the Ma'rib Dam collapse and became Byzantine foederati, serving as a buffer against Persian-allied Arab tribes. They played a crucial role in pre-Islamic Arabian politics and were rivals of the Lakhmid kingdom. The tribe was eventually absorbed into the broader Islamic conquests and lost political autonomy after the 7th century.

name: Banu Hanifa
legitimacyNotes: Classical Arab genealogists counted them as a Christian branch of Bani Bakr, but they led an independent existence prior to Islam. Later assimilated into Anizah tribe.
description: Banu Hanifa is an ancient Arab tribe that inhabited the area of al-Yamama in central Saudi Arabia. Members of the great Rabi'ah branch of North Arabian tribes, they were primarily sedentary farmers living in settlements along the wadis of eastern Nejd. The tribe played a significant role in early Islamic history, initially converting to Islam but then apostatizing under their own prophet Musaylima, leading to the Ridda Wars. After their defeat, they rejoined the Islamic state and later spread to various regions including Iraq and Egypt. The ruling House of Saud of Saudi Arabia are Hanifites, and according to King Salman Al Saud, the tribe eventually assimilated into the larger Anizah tribe.

subTribes.name: Anizzah
subTribes.legitimacyNotes: Well-documented blood lineage within the established Rabiʿa-Adnanite genealogical framework, with historical continuity from pre-Islamic times through modern era.
subTribes.ancestorStory: The classical Arab genealogists placed Anizah within the large Rabiʿa branch of the Adnanites alongside tribes of Abd al-Qays, Banu Bakr, Bani Hanifa, and Taghlib. Anizah's eponymous ancestor is described as a great uncle of all these related tribes, indicating they come from the same family and most likely one patriarch.
subTribes.traditionalEconomy: Mixed economy of pastoral nomadism and tribute collection. Nomadic branches practiced seasonal migration between northern Arabian steppes and Syria, while levying crops from oasis inhabitants and collecting khuwwa (brotherhood tribute) from Syrian farmers in exchange for protection.
subTribes.description: Anizah (Arabic: عنزة) is a major Arabian tribe with presence across the Arabian Peninsula, Upper Mesopotamia, and the Levant. Their existence as an autonomous tribal group predates Islam, and they are classified within the Rabiʿa branch of the Adnanites. The tribe has both nomadic and sedentary branches, with the nomadic branch living in northern Arabian steppes and the sedentary branch in Al-Yamama district of eastern Najd. They became prominent masters of oasis towns like Khaybar and Al-'Ula during the Ottoman era, collecting tribute from agricultural communities while maintaining seasonal migration patterns.

description: The Banu Hilal is a major Arab tribe from Najd in central Arabian Peninsula that migrated to the Maghreb in the 11th century, playing a crucial role in the Arabization of North Africa. They originated as nomadic Bedouins who ruled parts of Najd and campaigned in Iraq-Syria borderlands. The Fatimid Caliphate used them to punish the rebellious Zirids by dispatching them westward around 1050. Their migration, numbering between 150,000-300,000 people, fundamentally transformed the linguistic and cultural landscape of the Maghreb, spreading Bedouin Arabic and nomadic lifestyle across previously agricultural regions. They were organized into six main sub-tribes and became one of the most influential Arab tribal confederations in North African history.

description: The Bani Khalid were a powerful Arabian tribe that dominated eastern Arabia from the 17th to 19th centuries. They established the Bani Khalid Emirate in 1669 under Emir Barrak ibn Ghurayr, controlling the Al-Hasa and Al-Qatif regions after expelling Ottoman forces. The tribe's chieftainship was traditionally held by the Al Humaid clan. Known for their military prowess, they were staunch enemies of the emerging Saudi state and the Wahhabi movement. Their power declined after repeated defeats by Saudi-tribal alliances, with their rule ending definitively in 1830, though they briefly regained some authority under Ottoman patronage in 1874.

As part of the Tayy tribal system, they participated in the complex tribal politics and alliances of the region. Their historical significance is primarily documented in early Islamic historical sources, though they do not appear to have maintained distinct tribal identity in the modern Gulf states.

name: Banu Makhzum
legitimacyNotes: Prestigious Quraysh clan with strong pre-Islamic merchant tradition and early Islamic military leadership
ancestorStory: Makhzum ibn Yaqaza was a descendant of Fihr ibn Malik of Quraysh. The clan became known for their wealth, military prowess, and opposition to early Islam before eventually converting.
description: Banu Makhzum was one of the most prominent clans of the Quraysh tribe in pre-Islamic Mecca, known for their wealth from trade and their military leadership. They initially opposed the Prophet Muhammad and early Islam, with notable figures like Abu Jahl, but later converted and produced distinguished military commanders like Khalid ibn al-Walid. As a Meccan Quraysh clan, they were part of the broader tribal structure that later influenced Islamic expansion. Their historical significance is primarily tied to early Islamic history rather than modern GCC tribal politics.

name: Banu Manhib
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal lineage and genealogical recognition
ancestorStory: Manhib is considered the eponymous ancestor of the tribe. The lineage traces back through established Arabian genealogical traditions.
description: Banu Manhib was a historical Arabian tribe with Qahtani origins mentioned in classical Arab genealogical sources. They were part of the broader tribal structure of the Arabian Peninsula during the pre-Islamic and early Islamic periods. Like many smaller Arabian tribes, their specific territorial boundaries and political activities are not extensively documented in major historical chronicles. Their current status and presence in the modern Gulf states is unclear from available historical records.

name: Banu Al-Mashruki
legitimacyNotes: traditional tribal lineage and customary leadership
ancestorStory: Named after their ancestor Al-Mashrouq, who was known for his eastern migrations. The name relates to 'mashriq' meaning east or sunrise.
description: Banu Al-Mashruki were a tribal branch historically associated with the eastern Arabian Peninsula. They were part of the broader Qahtani tribal confederation and engaged primarily in nomadic pastoralism and trade routes. The tribe's name suggests connections to eastern migrations or territories. They are primarily of historical significance with limited contemporary documentation.

relations.context: part of broader Qahtani confederation

name: Banu Muayiya
legitimacyNotes: Descent from Umayyad lineage and traditional tribal authority
ancestorStory: Named after Muawiya ibn Abi Sufyan, the founder of the Umayyad Caliphate. The tribal branch claims descent from this Umayyad lineage.
description: Banu Muayiya is a tribal branch that claims descent from the Umayyad caliphal lineage, specifically tracing their name to Muawiya ibn Abi Sufyan. They were part of the broader Adnani tribal confederation in the Arabian Peninsula. The tribe's prominence was largely tied to the early Islamic period when Umayyad authority was at its peak. Their historical presence in the Gulf region was limited, with most branches settling in the Levant and other parts of the former Umayyad territories.

relations.context: Both claim Adnani descent and early Islamic connections

name: Banu Mustaliq
legitimacyNotes: Pre-Islamic Arabian tribe with documented presence during Prophet Muhammad's time
ancestorStory: Descended from Khuza'a, an ancient Arabian tribal confederation. The tribe was established as a distinct branch of the larger Khuza'i confederation in pre-Islamic Arabia.
description: Banu Mustaliq was a pre-Islamic Arabian tribe belonging to the Khuza'a confederation, primarily located in the Hijaz region. They are historically significant for their encounter with the Muslim community during the expedition of al-Muraysi in 627 CE, which resulted in their integration into the early Islamic state. The tribe was known for their pastoral lifestyle and control over water sources in their territory. Their historical presence is well-documented in early Islamic sources, though they do not appear to have maintained distinct tribal identity in the modern Gulf states.

name: Banu Nabhan (Aswadan)
legitimacyNotes: Branch of the larger Tayy confederation, derived from pre-Islamic Arabian tribal structure
ancestorStory: Nabhan was a descendant of the Tayy tribe, with Aswadan being a specific lineage branch. The tribe traces its origins to the ancient Tayy confederation of northern Arabia.
description: Banu Nabhan (Aswadan) was a tribal branch of the ancient Tayy confederation, one of the prominent Qahtani Arab tribes of pre-Islamic and early Islamic Arabia. They were traditionally nomadic pastoralists who migrated across the northern Arabian Peninsula. As part of the larger Tayy tribal system, they participated in the complex tribal politics of early Arabia and some branches may have migrated to various parts of the Gulf region over time. The tribe is now considered historical, with descendants likely absorbed into other tribal groupings.

name: Banu Nu'aym
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal leadership based on genealogical descent and consensus
ancestorStory: Nu'aym ibn Hammam was the progenitor of the tribe, descending from the ancient Arab lineages of the Arabian Peninsula.
description: Banu Nu'aym is a prominent Arab tribe with historical presence across the Arabian Peninsula, particularly in Oman and the UAE. They established themselves as one of the significant tribal groups in the region during the medieval period. The tribe played important roles in various political alliances and conflicts throughout Gulf history, maintaining their traditional pastoral lifestyle while adapting to changing political circumstances. Today, members of the tribe are found across multiple GCC states, with many having settled into modern occupations while maintaining tribal identity.

Political alignment in Omani tribal politics

name: Banu Qays ibn Tha'laba
legitimacyNotes: Genealogical descent from Qays ibn Tha'laba within the Adnani tribal structure
ancestorStory: Qays ibn Tha'laba was a tribal ancestor whose descendants formed various branches across the Arabian Peninsula. The lineage traces back through the Adnani genealogical framework.
description: Banu Qays ibn Tha'laba represents a tribal branch within the broader Adnani genealogical system of pre-Islamic Arabia. This tribal grouping was part of the complex network of Arabian tribes that existed before and during the early Islamic period. Like many pre-Islamic tribal branches, they were primarily involved in nomadic pastoralism and tribal warfare. Their historical significance is preserved mainly through genealogical records rather than specific political achievements in the Gulf region.

relations.context: Both trace descent through the Adnani genealogical framework

name: Banu Quda'a
legitimacyNotes: Ancient tribal confederation claiming descent from Quda'a ibn Ma'add, with traditional authority over constituent tribes
ancestorStory: Quda'a was said to be the son of Ma'add and brother to Nizar, though some genealogists disputed whether he was Qahtani or Adnani. The confederation formed around tribes claiming descent from his various sons.
description: Banu Quda'a was a major pre-Islamic tribal confederation that included numerous tribes across the Arabian Peninsula and Levant. They were known for their migrations and military prowess, with constituent tribes settling from southern Arabia to Syria. The confederation played significant roles in pre-Islamic politics and early Islamic conquests. Most constituent tribes eventually integrated into other formations or developed independent identities, making the confederation primarily of historical significance.

Mahri lineage traces back through Quda'a confederation

name: Banu Sa'd
legitimacyNotes: Genealogical descent from Sa'd ibn Bakr ibn Hawazin
ancestorStory: Sa'd ibn Bakr was a prominent figure among the Hawazin tribal confederation. His descendants became known for their nobility and were chosen as foster families for important figures including Prophet Muhammad.
description: Banu Sa'd were a prominent pre-Islamic Arabian tribe, part of the larger Hawazin confederation, historically based in the Najd region. They are most famously known as the foster tribe of Prophet Muhammad, with Halima al-Sa'diyya serving as his wet nurse. The tribe played significant roles in early Islamic history and later migrations across the Arabian Peninsula. Many of their descendants integrated into other tribal confederations or settled in various parts of the Gulf region.

relations.context: Muhammad's foster mother Halimah al-Sa'diyah was from Banu Sa'd, a subdivision of Hawazin

name: Banu Sahm
legitimacyNotes: Ancient Meccan clan with historical religious and commercial authority in pre-Islamic Arabia
ancestorStory: Sahm ibn Murrah was a prominent ancestor of Quraysh. The clan held custodianship of divination arrows at the Kaaba in pre-Islamic Mecca.
description: Banu Sahm was a prominent clan of the Quraysh tribe based in Mecca during pre-Islamic times. They held the hereditary role of custodians of the divination arrows (azlam) at the Kaaba and were involved in Meccan trade networks. Notable members included Amr ibn al-As, the Arab general who conquered Egypt. The clan's influence diminished after the early Islamic conquests as political power shifted away from traditional Meccan tribal structures.

name: Banu Al-Sakasek
description: No verified historical records found for Banu Al-Sakasek in Arabian Peninsula tribal genealogical sources or Gulf historical documentation. This tribal name does not appear in established references for GCC tribal structures or Arabian tribal confederations. Further genealogical research would be required to verify the existence and historical significance of this tribal designation.

name: Banu Shayban
legitimacyNotes: Ancient Arabian tribe with established genealogical records and historical presence
ancestorStory: Descended from Shayban ibn Tha'laba ibn Ukashah ibn Sa'b ibn Ali ibn Bakr ibn Wa'il. The tribe traces its lineage to the northern Arabian tribal confederation of Rabi'a.
description: Banu Shayban was a prominent pre-Islamic and early Islamic Arabian tribe belonging to the Bakr ibn Wa'il confederation. They were historically based in northeastern Arabia and lower Mesopotamia, playing significant roles in pre-Islamic conflicts and early Islamic conquests. The tribe was known for their warrior culture and participated in major battles including the famous Battle of Dhi Qar against the Sassanians. While their direct tribal structure has largely merged with other groups over time, their descendants are found across the Arabian Peninsula and Iraq.

relations.context: Both tribes belong to the broader Rabi'a tribal confederation
Historical presence in lower Mesopotamia and eastern Arabia

name: Banu Al-Sukun
legitimacyNotes: Ancient Yemeni tribal branch with documented pre-Islamic genealogy
ancestorStory: Al-Sukun ibn Ashras was a prominent ancestor from the Kindah confederation. The tribe traces its lineage through the ancient Kahlan branch of Qahtani Arabs.
description: Banu Al-Sukun was a significant tribal branch of the Kindah confederation, originating from Yemen and part of the broader Qahtani lineage. They were prominent in pre-Islamic Arabia and played roles in early Islamic conquests and migrations. Like many ancient Arabian tribes, they dispersed across the peninsula during various migration waves, with some branches eventually settling in areas that would become part of modern GCC states. The tribe is primarily of historical significance, with their descendants integrated into larger tribal confederations over the centuries.

relations.context: Both trace descent from Qahtani lineage

name: Banu Sulaym
legitimacyNotes: Ancient Arabian tribe with well-documented genealogy tracing to Adnanite lineage
ancestorStory: Sulaym ibn Mansur was a descendant of Qays Aylan from the Adnanite branch. The tribe became one of the major Arabian tribes with extensive migrations across the Arabian Peninsula.
description: Banu Sulaym was a major Arabian tribe of Adnanite origin that played significant roles in pre-Islamic and early Islamic history. Originally based in the Najd and Hijaz regions, various branches migrated extensively across Arabia and North Africa. Some branches settled in eastern Arabia and maintained presence in areas that would later become part of modern Gulf states. The tribe is historically significant for its participation in early Islamic conquests and tribal confederations.

Al Bin Ali clan traces lineage to Banu Sulaym rather than Anizah

name: Amr
legitimacyNotes: Lineage authority through descent from Tamim ibn Murr
ancestorStory: Amr was one of the sons of Tamim ibn Murr, from whom this branch of Banu Tamim traces its descent. The lineage represents one of the major subdivisions within the larger Tamimi confederation.
description: Banu Tamim Amr represents a major tribal branch within the larger Banu Tamim confederation, one of the most prominent Adnani Arab tribes. They were historically based in the Najd region of central Arabia and played significant roles in pre-Islamic tribal politics and early Islamic conquests. As part of the broader Tamimi tribal system, they maintained traditional Bedouin culture centered on camel herding and tribal warfare. Many descendants have integrated into modern Gulf societies, particularly in Saudi Arabia and other GCC states.

relations.context: Historical alliance with Saudi state in Najd
Common descent from Adnan through Tamim lineage

name: Banu Taym
legitimacyNotes: Ancient Quraishi lineage tracing to Taym ibn Murra, recognized tribal nobility in pre-Islamic and early Islamic periods
ancestorStory: Taym ibn Murra was an ancestor of the Quraysh tribe, giving rise to this branch that became prominent in Mecca. The tribe produced notable figures including Abu Bakr al-Siddiq, the first Caliph of Islam.
description: Banu Taym was a prominent sub-tribe of Quraysh based in Mecca, most famous for producing Abu Bakr al-Siddiq, the Prophet Muhammad's close companion and first Caliph. They were influential merchants in pre-Islamic Arabia and played a crucial role in early Islamic expansion. The tribe's significance was primarily centered in the Hijaz region rather than the Gulf states. Their historical importance lies in their contribution to early Islamic leadership and their role in the Rashidun Caliphate.

relations.context: Both trace descent through Adnanite lineage

name: Banu Tha'labah
legitimacyNotes: Genealogical descent from Tha'labah ibn 'Amr, recognized lineage within larger tribal confederations
ancestorStory: Descended from Tha'labah ibn 'Amr, a prominent figure in pre-Islamic Arabian tribal genealogies. The tribe traces its origins to the ancient Arabian tribal system of the central and northern Peninsula.
description: Banu Tha'labah was a pre-Islamic Arabian tribe with Adnani lineage, primarily based in the central and northern Arabian Peninsula. They were known as skilled pastoralists and warriors who participated in the tribal conflicts and alliances of pre-Islamic Arabia. The tribe played various roles in early Islamic expansion and gradually integrated into larger tribal confederations. Most branches of Banu Tha'labah eventually merged with other tribal groups or settled into sedentary communities across the Arabian Peninsula.

relations.context: Part of the broader Adnani genealogical framework

name: Banu Thaqif
legitimacyNotes: Ancient Arabian tribe with documented pre-Islamic lineage and historical prominence in Hijaz
ancestorStory: Thaqif ibn Munabbih was the eponymous ancestor of the tribe, descended from the Hawazin tribal confederation. The tribe established itself in the mountainous region around Ta'if in the Hijaz.
description: Banu Thaqif was a powerful pre-Islamic Arabian tribe based in Ta'if in the Hijaz region. They were known for their wealth from trade and their initial resistance to Islam, being one of the last major tribes to convert. The tribe produced notable figures including Al-Hajjaj ibn Yusuf, the Umayyad governor. While historically significant in Arabian Peninsula politics, their primary influence was in the Hijaz rather than the eastern Arabian Gulf regions that comprise the modern GCC states.

Both claim Adnani lineage through broader Arabian tribal genealogies

name: Banu Thu'al

ancestorStory: Thu'al was a descendant of Tayy ibn Udad, establishing this branch of the greater Tayy confederation. The lineage traces back to the Qahtani tribal roots of southern Arabia.
description: Banu Thu'al was a branch of the Tayy tribal confederation, one of the major Qahtani tribes of northern Arabia. They were part of the broader Tayy migration patterns that spread across the Arabian Peninsula during pre-Islamic and early Islamic periods. As a component of Tayy, they participated in the complex tribal dynamics of northern and central Arabia. Their historical significance is primarily as part of the larger Tayy tribal structure rather than as an independent political entity.

name: Banu Toghlib
legitimacyNotes: Ancient Arabian tribal lineage with pre-Islamic nobility and Christian heritage
ancestorStory: Descended from Taghlib ibn Wa'il ibn Qasit, one of the major northern Arabian tribal ancestors. The tribe was known for its strength and pride, with the saying 'more arrogant than Taghlib' becoming proverbial.
description: Banu Taghlib was a major pre-Islamic Arabian tribe descended from Taghlib ibn Wa'il, primarily based in northern Arabia and Mesopotamia. They were predominantly Christian and known for their fierce independence and military prowess. The tribe played a significant role in pre-Islamic Arabian politics and the early Islamic conquests, though they gradually integrated into the broader Islamic community. Their descendants are scattered across various Arab regions today, with limited direct presence in the modern GCC states.

relations.context: Both descend from Qasit branch of northern Arabian tribes

name: Banu Yam
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal authority based on Qahtani lineage and historical control of trade routes
ancestorStory: Yam ibn Asbah was a descendant of Hamdan ibn Zaid from the Qahtani branch. The tribe traces its origins to the ancient kingdoms of South Arabia.
description: Banu Yam is a major Qahtani tribal confederation originally from the Najran region of southern Arabia, with branches extending into Yemen. Historically one of the most powerful tribes in the Empty Quarter, they controlled important trade routes and maintained extensive grazing territories. The tribe migrated extensively across the Arabian Peninsula, with some sections settling in various parts of the Gulf region. Today they remain active with populations in Saudi Arabia, Yemen, and some Gulf states.

name: Banu Yashkur

ancestorStory: Yashkur was an ancestor from the Bakr ibn Wa'il tribal group. The tribe traces its lineage through the ancient Rabi'a confederation.
description: Banu Yashkur was an ancient Arabian tribe belonging to the Bakr ibn Wa'il confederation, part of the larger Rabi'a tribal group. They were historically present in eastern Arabia and participated in the tribal dynamics of the pre-Islamic and early Islamic periods. The tribe was known for their nomadic lifestyle and tribal warfare typical of Arabian Peninsula tribes. Their historical significance relates primarily to early Arabian tribal genealogy and the formation of tribal confederations that would later influence Gulf region politics.

name: Banu Zuhra
legitimacyNotes: Prestigious Qurayshi lineage from pre-Islamic Mecca
ancestorStory: Zuhra ibn Kilab was the ancestor of this noble Qurayshi clan. The tribe is most famous for being the maternal lineage of Prophet Muhammad through his mother Aminah bint Wahb.
description: Banu Zuhra was a prominent clan of the Quraysh tribe based in pre-Islamic Mecca. They are historically significant as the maternal tribe of Prophet Muhammad, as his mother Aminah bint Wahb belonged to this lineage. The tribe was involved in the traditional Meccan trade networks and held respected status among the Quraysh. While their direct political influence was primarily in the Hijaz region, their descendants and affiliated lineages spread throughout the Arabian Peninsula including areas of the modern Gulf states.

name: Bariq
legitimacyNotes: Qahtani tribal lineage with established territory in southwestern Arabian Peninsula
ancestorStory: Bariq traces its lineage to Bariq ibn Uday ibn Haritha ibn Amr Muzayqiya ibn Amir Ma' al-Sama ibn Haritha al-Ghatraf ibn Imru' al-Qays ibn Tha'laba ibn Mazin ibn al-Azd. The tribe established itself in the mountainous regions between Hijaz and Asir.
description: Bariq is a Qahtani tribe historically based in the mountainous regions between Hijaz and Asir in southwestern Saudi Arabia. They are known for their strong tribal organization and have maintained their territorial presence for centuries. The tribe has traditional settlements in areas like Wadi Bariq and surrounding highlands. While primarily based outside the main GCC states, some branches have historical connections to broader Arabian tribal networks.

relations.context: Both trace descent from Qahtani tribal confederation

name: Beja peoples
description: The Beja are a Cushitic-speaking nomadic confederation primarily based in the Red Sea hills of Sudan, Eritrea, and southeastern Egypt. They are pastoralists who have historically controlled trade routes between the Nile Valley and the Red Sea coast. While not originally from the Arabian Peninsula, some Beja groups have intermarried with Arabian tribes and established presence in parts of the Arabian Peninsula through historical migrations and trade connections. They maintain their distinct cultural identity while having connections to Arab tribal networks.

relations.context: In Egypt, Rabi'a tribesmen inter-married with indigenous Beja peoples

name: Bithyni
description: The Bithyni appears to reference a historical group that may be connected to ancient Bithynia in Asia Minor rather than Arabian Peninsula tribal genealogy. There is insufficient reliable documentation of a tribe by this name in traditional Arabian or Gulf tribal histories. Without clear historical sources linking this name to Arabian Peninsula tribal structures, their role in GCC history cannot be established. Further research into alternative transliterations or regional variations would be needed for accurate identification.

name: Dahaminah
description: The Dahaminah are a minor tribe from Ras Al Khaimah and Fujairah in the United Arab Emirates (UAE). They originally inhabited the area of Wadi Qor and at the turn of the 19th century had a population of approximately 150 members who co-habited with members of the Manai'i tribe in settlements along the Wadi Qor.

relations.context: Co-habited in settlements along Wadi Qor at turn of 19th century

name: Dar Alearabi
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal lineage and customary leadership
ancestorStory: Named after their ancestor Al-Arabi, part of the broader tribal migrations in the Arabian Peninsula.
description: Dar Al-Arabi is a tribal branch with historical presence in the Arabian Peninsula. The tribe's name suggests Arab lineage and traditional nomadic pastoral lifestyle. Limited historical documentation exists about their specific activities or territorial control. Their current status appears to be largely historical with possible integration into larger tribal confederations.

name: Daramikah
description: The Daramikah are an Arabian tribe and a branch of the widespread Dhawahir of the United Arab Emirates (UAE) and Oman. They have given their name to a small watchtower located in the Qattara Oasis in Al Ain, traditionally held by the semi-nomadic Dhawahir. The tower protected the falaj irrigation system that gave life to the oasis. In the latter part of the 19th century, the Al bu Falah of Abu Dhabi bought out much of the property previously owned by the Dhawahir, who continued to cultivate the land in various oases.

relations.context: Daramikah are a branch of the widespread Dhawahir
Al bu Falah bought out much of the property previously owned by the Dhawahir in the latter part of the 19th century

name: Darawishah
legitimacyNotes: traditional tribal structure with established lineage claims
description: The Darawishah are a tribal group found in parts of the Arabian Peninsula. Limited historical documentation makes it difficult to establish their precise origins, traditional territories, or major historical role. They appear to maintain tribal identity in contemporary times though their specific influence and activities in GCC regional affairs remain unclear from available sources.

name: Dawasir
legitimacyNotes: Al-Dawasir originates from a confederation of tribes of the Azd and the Banu Toghlib (Arabic: بنو تغلب), also known as Taghlib ibn Wa'il. The 'original' Dawasir are believed to be descendants from Doser (military settlement) in Najd, Saudi Arabia.
ancestorStory: There are various theories surrounding the definition and origin of the term 'Dawasir'. The two most popular theories are that it was derived from either the name of the tribe's purported forebear Dosser or the eponymous Arabic word which translates to 'soldiers'.
description: The Dawasir are an Arab tribe in Saudi Arabia, Bahrain, Qatar, and other Gulf states, with their main base in southern Najd, specifically in Wadi Al-Dawasir in Saudi Arabia. The Dawasir tribe is among the most powerful and influential tribes of Arabia, as they are the maternal uncles of much of the House of Saud family. The tribe is considered one of the largest Arab tribes in terms of numbers, as their number exceeds 6 million.

relations.context: Members of the tribe played key roles in supporting the early Saudi state militarily and politically. These connections were solidified through intermarriage, most notably between King AbdulAziz Al Saud and Hussa bint Ahmed Al Sudairi
The Dawasir were the second largest and most powerful tribe after the Utub in Bahrain, maintaining complex relationship with Al Khalifa rulers

name: Daws
legitimacyNotes: Ancient Arabian tribe with well-documented pre-Islamic lineage and Islamic period prominence
ancestorName: Daws ibn Udathah ibn Zayd al-Lat
ancestorStory: Daws traces its lineage to Daws ibn Udathah, a descendant of Zayd al-Lat from the Azd tribal confederation. The tribe was established in the mountainous regions of the Hejaz before the rise of Islam.
description: Daws was an ancient Arabian tribe from the Azd confederation, historically based in the mountainous regions between Mecca and Yemen. They are most famous for producing Abu Hurairah, one of the most prominent companions of Prophet Muhammad and narrator of hadith. The tribe converted to Islam during the Prophet's lifetime and played a role in early Islamic conquests. While historically significant in Arabian Peninsula history, their direct relevance to modern GCC tribal politics is limited as they were primarily based in the Hejaz region.

relations.context: Both trace ancestry to Qahtani Arab origins through Azd confederation

name: Dhahuriyiin
description: The Dhahuriyiin appear to be a smaller tribal group or clan historically present in the Arabian Peninsula. Limited historical records suggest they maintained alliance relationships with other tribal groups, particularly the Habus. Their current status and specific geographic distribution within the GCC region remains unclear from available sources.

relations.context: Associated as fellow Hajar Mountain tribes with shared cultural similarities

name: Dhawahir
legitimacyNotes: Appears to be a genuine tribal lineage with historical continuity in the Al Ain/Buraimi region, not a political confederation
traditionalEconomy: date cultivation, sheep herding, camel breeding, charcoal trading
description: The Dhawahir is a prominent tribe of the United Arab Emirates with their main centre at the Buraimi Oasis and Al Ain. They have historically maintained a strong alliance with the Al Nahyan ruling family of Abu Dhabi and the Bani Yas confederation. At the turn of the 20th century, they owned much of the cultivated land in Al Ain oasis (except Buraimi village itself) and numbered around 4,500 people. The tribe was traditionally involved in date cultivation, sheep herding, camel breeding, and charcoal trading. Their fertile woodlands, irrigated by an effective falaj system, supported their wood-burning industry.

name: Dhu Asbah
legitimacyNotes: Ancient Himyarite tribal legitimacy through South Arabian genealogical traditions
ancestorStory: Descended from Asbah ibn Dhu Raydan, a branch of the ancient Himyarite kings of Yemen. The tribe traces its origins to the pre-Islamic Himyarite kingdom in southern Arabia.
description: Dhu Asbah was an ancient South Arabian tribe belonging to the Himyarite confederation that ruled much of Yemen before Islam. They were part of the broader Dhu Raydan tribal complex associated with the Himyarite royal house. The tribe was primarily based in the highlands of Yemen and played a role in the political structure of pre-Islamic South Arabia. Like many ancient Yemeni tribes, their direct lineage became absorbed into later tribal formations following the Islamic conquests and subsequent migrations.

relations.context: Both trace descent to Qahtani South Arabian origins

name: dhū-Raydān
legitimacyNotes: Ancient South Arabian royal dynasty that ruled parts of Yemen and southern Arabia
ancestorStory: Dhū-Raydān derives its name from Raydan, referring to the royal palace or capital of the Himyarite kings. The dynasty claimed descent from ancient South Arabian nobility.
description: Dhū-Raydān was an ancient South Arabian royal dynasty associated with the Himyarite kingdom that controlled much of Yemen and southern Arabia. They ruled from their capital at Zafar and later Sanaa, controlling important trade routes between the Indian Ocean and the Mediterranean. The dynasty is known from numerous South Arabian inscriptions where rulers styled themselves as 'King of Saba, Dhū-Raydān, Hadramawt and Yamanat.' The dynasty declined in the 6th century CE with the rise of Islamic conquests and has no direct connection to modern GCC tribal structures.

relations.context: Both trace descent to ancient Qahtani lineages of South Arabia

name: Dhu'l-Kala
legitimacyNotes: Ancient Himyarite royal lineage with historical sovereignty in Yemen
ancestorStory: Named after their ancestor who ruled parts of ancient Yemen and held the title 'Dhu'l-Kala' meaning 'possessor of the fortress'. They were among the noble houses of the Himyarite kingdom.
description: Dhu'l-Kala was an ancient Qahtani tribe from the Himyarite nobility of Yemen, known for their royal lineage and territorial control in pre-Islamic Arabia. They were among the established ruling houses of South Arabia before Islam and participated in the early Islamic conquests. The tribe's influence was primarily centered in Yemen rather than the modern GCC states. Their historical significance lies in their role as one of the ancient Arab royal families that bridged the pre-Islamic and Islamic periods.

relations.context: Both descend from Qahtani lineage

description: Himyar was a powerful ancient kingdom and polity in the southern highlands of Yemen that existed from 110 BCE until the early 6th century CE. The kingdom was a confederation of tribes that created a wealthy, sophisticated society with rich trade networks linking the Mediterranean, Middle East, India, and East Africa. Himyar conquered neighboring kingdoms including Saba', Qataban, and Haḍramaut, uniting southern Arabia for the first time by 300 CE. The kingdom underwent significant religious transformations, converting from South Arabian polytheism to Judaism in the 4th century under Malkikarib Yuhamin, and later to Christianity after the Aksumite conquest in 530 CE.
