legitimacyNotes: A major tribal confederation of ancient Saba', with genuine historical presence in Yemen before forced migration due to Himyarite pressure
ancestorName: Kahlan bin Saba bin Yishjab bin Yarub bin Qahtan
ancestorStory: Descended from Kahlan bin Saba bin Yishjab bin Yarub bin Qahtan, making them one of the main Qahtani confederations originating from ancient Saba' kingdom in Yemen.
peakPowerEra: Ancient Sabaean period, before 2nd century BC
traditionalEconomy: Highland pastoralism and agriculture in Yemen, later nomadic pastoralism during migrations
description: Kahlan was one of the main tribal confederations of Saba' in Ancient Yemen, descended from Kahlan bin Saba bin Yishjab bin Yarub bin Qahtan. Originally dominant in the Yemeni highlands, they were forced out by Himyar in the 2nd century BC and migrated northwards throughout Arabia after the destruction of the Marib Dam in the 3rd century AD, eventually reaching as far as Mesopotamia and Syria.

subTribes.name: Azd
subTribes.legitimacyNotes: Ancient Arabian tribe with documented pre-Islamic inscriptions from 210-230 CE confirming their territorial presence. Genetic evidence suggests J-BY74 haplogroup associated with Azdite tribes originated in Northern Arabia or the Levant.
subTribes.ancestorStory: In the 3rd century CE, the Azd branched into four sub-branches, each led by one of the sons of Muzayqiya
subTribes.traditionalEconomy: trade and conquest - chief merchant group of Oman and Al-Ubulla, organized trading diaspora with settlements on coasts of Kirman and Makran
subTribes.description: The Azd (Arabic: أَزْد), or Al-Azd (Arabic: ٱلْأَزْد), is an ancient Arabian tribe originating from Northern Arabia. The lands of Azd occupied an area west of Bisha and Al Bahah in what is today Saudi Arabia. The Azd tribal group originally lived in Yemen according to medieval folklore, until the collapse of the Ma'rib Dam in the late 3rd century CE when they began emigrating to other parts of the Arabian Peninsula.

subTribes.name: Tayy
subTribes.legitimacyNotes: Real blood lineage traced to Qahtanite origins, though Ibn Khaldun noted that Tayy intermarried with non-Arabs and did not preserve lineage purity strictly
subTribes.ancestorName: Julhumah ibn Udad (known as Tayy or Tayyi)
subTribes.ancestorStory: According to early Arab genealogists, Julhumah ibn Udad was the progenitor of the Tayy. His surname 'Ṭayyiʾ' supposedly derived from the Arabic word 'ṭawā' meaning 'to plaster' because he was said to be the first to have plastered the walls of a well. His ancestry was traced to Kahlan ibn Saba ibn Ya'rub, great-grandson of Qahtan.
subTribes.traditionalEconomy: pastoralism and control of trade routes
subTribes.description: The Tayy are a large and ancient Arab tribe, among whose descendants today are the tribes of Bani Sakher and Shammar. Originally based in Yemen, they migrated to northern Arabia in the late 2nd century CE and settled in the Shammar and Salma Mountains, which became known as Jabal Tayy. They established relations with both the Sasanian and Byzantine empires, supplanted the Lakhmids as rulers of al-Hirah in the 610s, and played significant roles throughout Islamic history. The tribe was split during the Fasad War, with the Jadila branch converting to Christianity and migrating to Syria, while the Ghawth branch remained in Jabal Tayy.

subTribes.name: Al Murrah
subTribes.description: The Al Murrah are an Arab tribe descended from the Banu Yam tribe who historically were camel-herding nomads controlling and travelling through vast areas of the Arabian Peninsula. They comprise approximately 15,000 individuals with seven clans making up the Saudi branch, and historically made up a large proportion of Qatar's ethnic population. The tribe has been involved in significant political events in Qatar, including supporting former Emir Sheikh Khalifa Al Thani and later attempting a failed coup against Sheikh Hamad Al Thani in 1996.

subTribes.name: Shammar
subTribes.legitimacyNotes: A genuine tribal confederation with three main branches (Abdah, Al-Aslam, and Sinjarah) that evolved from the larger Tayy tribal group. The name change from Tayy to Shammar occurred by the 14th century when they became known in literature as Shammar.
subTribes.ancestorStory: Descended from the Tayy tribe which migrated from Yemen to northern Arabian Peninsula. Led by Usma bin Luai, the Tayy invaded the mountains of Ajā and Salma from Banu Assad and Banu Tamim in northern Arabia in their exodus from Yemen in 115 CE.
subTribes.traditionalEconomy: nomadic camel-herding, horse-breeding, city-dwelling in Ha'il, and agriculture in oases
subTribes.description: The Shammar is a tribal Arab Qahtanite confederation descended from the Tayy, which migrated into northern Arabian Peninsula from Yemen in the second century. It is the largest branch of the Tayy and one of the largest and most influential Arab tribes. The historical seat of tribal leadership is in Ḥaʼil, and at its peak around the 1850s, the Shammar ruled much of central and northern Arabia from Riyadh to the frontiers of Syria and Upper Mesopotamia.

name: Kawahla
legitimacyNotes: Traditional Bedouin tribal authority based on ancestral lineage and customary law
description: The Kawahla are a Bedouin tribe with historical presence in the Arabian Peninsula. They share migratory patterns and routes with the Manasir tribe, indicating traditional pastoral movements across tribal territories. Like many Arabian tribes, they followed seasonal grazing patterns and maintained traditional Bedouin social structures. Their current status and specific territorial associations within the GCC states require further documentation.

name: al-Khabra
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal lineage and pastoral territory rights
description: Al-Khabra is a minor tribal group historically associated with the Arabian Peninsula's pastoral traditions. Limited historical documentation exists about this particular tribal branch, suggesting they may have been a smaller subdivision of a larger tribal confederation. Their activities appear to have been primarily focused on traditional nomadic pastoralism in the interior regions. Current status and contemporary presence in GCC states is unclear from available historical sources.

name: Khanazirah
description: Khanazirah appears to be a minor tribal group or branch historically present in the Arabian Peninsula. Limited historical records exist about this particular tribal entity, and their specific role in Gulf regional politics or economy remains unclear. They may represent a sub-branch or clan within a larger tribal confederation. Current status and activities are not well documented in major historical sources.

name: Khath'am
legitimacyNotes: Ancient Qahtani tribal confederation with documented pre-Islamic lineage
ancestorStory: Khath'am traces ancestry to Khath'am ibn Anmar ibn Arash ibn Amr ibn al-Ghauth ibn Nabt ibn Malik ibn Zaid ibn Kahlan. The tribe formed a major confederation in the southwestern Arabian Peninsula.
description: Khath'am is an ancient Qahtani tribal confederation historically based in the southwestern Arabian Peninsula, particularly in the regions between Najran and the Asir mountains. They were prominent in pre-Islamic and early Islamic periods, with documented participation in the Ridda Wars and early Islamic conquests. The tribe maintained traditional territories in what is now southern Saudi Arabia and has historical connections to other major southwestern Arabian tribes. Today, Khath'am descendants are found across the Arabian Peninsula, including in GCC states.

relations.context: Ghamid assisted Khath'am against Banu Al-Harith despite previous problems between them
Both trace lineage to Qahtani ancestry

name: Khawatir
legitimacyNotes: The Khawatir are described as a legitimate subsection of the Na'im tribe, indicating a genuine blood lineage relationship rather than a political confederation.
ancestorStory: <UNKNOWN>
traditionalEconomy: Bedouin pastoralism with camels, sheep, goats, cattle, and date palm cultivation
description: The Khawatir is an Arab tribe of the United Arab Emirates, forming one of three subsections of the Na'im tribe. A Bedouin tribe whose traditional territory stretched from Buraimi north to the Jiri Plain, they mostly settled around the Buraimi oasis. They adopted Wahhabism in the 19th century in response to encroachment by the Al Bu Falah and their Manasir allies. The tribe frequently came into conflict with the Sharqiyin of Fujairah but would ally with them against the powerful Qawasim of Sharjah and Ras Al Khaimah. They also settled in Dhaid in Sharjah and were involved in various political disputes and alliances throughout their history.

description: Khawlaniyah al-Qahtaniyah Harb represents a Qahtani tribal branch that became integrated within the broader Harb confederation. Historically based in the Hejaz region, they maintained their distinct Qahtani identity while participating in the larger Harb tribal structure. The tribe followed traditional Bedouin pastoral economy and participated in the complex intertribal relationships of western Arabian Peninsula. Their current status is largely historical, with descendants likely absorbed into other tribal groupings.

relations.context: Both claim descent from Qahtani ancestors

name: Khuza'a
legitimacyNotes: Ancient Arabian tribe with pre-Islamic nobility and custodianship of Mecca
ancestorStory: Named after Khuza'a ibn Amr, the tribe is said to have separated (khuza'a meaning 'to separate') from other Azd tribes during their migration from Yemen. They established themselves in the Hijaz region around Mecca.
description: Khuza'a was a prominent pre-Islamic Arabian tribe that controlled Mecca and its surrounding areas before the rise of Quraysh. They were the custodians of the Kaaba for several centuries and played a crucial role in Arabian trade routes. The tribe allied with Prophet Muhammad during the conquest of Mecca, which was partly triggered by a conflict between Khuza'a and their rivals. While historically significant in Arabian Peninsula history, they are not prominently active in modern GCC tribal politics, having been absorbed into larger tribal confederations over time.

name: Kiawan
description: Kiawan appears to be a tribal name with limited documented historical presence in major Arabian Peninsula genealogical sources. Without reliable historical documentation, details about their origins, territorial associations, or role in Gulf regional politics cannot be definitively established. The name does not appear prominently in well-documented tribal confederations or major historical accounts of the GCC region. Further research would be needed to establish their historical significance and current status.

name: Kinana
legitimacyNotes: Ancient Arabian tribe with noble lineage tracing to Adnan
ancestorStory: Kinana was the son of Khuzayma ibn Mudrika, establishing one of the most prominent tribes of the Hijaz. The tribe became the parent lineage of Quraysh and gained prestige through this connection.
description: Kinana was a major ancient Arabian tribe of Adnani lineage based in the Hijaz region, particularly around Mecca and the surrounding areas. They are historically significant as the parent tribe of Quraysh, the tribe of Prophet Muhammad, which elevated their status considerably. The tribe played important roles in pre-Islamic Arabian politics and early Islamic history through their Qurayshi descendants. While the main tribal identity became subsumed under Quraysh and other sub-tribes, their genealogical significance remains paramount in Arabian tribal heritage.

relations.context: Quraysh descended from Kinana tribe, fought together in Fijar War
Some Saudi ruling family genealogies trace connections through Adnani lineage

name: Kingdom of Kinda
legitimacyNotes: Ancient Arabian kingdom with royal lineage claiming descent from Kahlan
ancestorStory: Kinda descended from Kahlan through Afsa, establishing one of the most prominent pre-Islamic Arabian kingdoms. The tribe produced kings who ruled over various Arabian tribes in Najd and Yemen.
description: The Kingdom of Kinda was one of the most important pre-Islamic Arabian kingdoms, ruling over much of central and eastern Arabia from the 5th-6th centuries CE. Based primarily in Najd, they extended their influence from Yemen to the borders of the Sassanian Empire, serving as kings over various tribal confederations. The Kindite dynasty produced famous rulers like Hujr ibn Amr and his sons, and the poet-prince Imru al-Qays. While the kingdom fragmented before Islam, Kindite descendants remain scattered across the Arabian Peninsula, including some presence in modern GCC states.

relations.context: Both trace descent from Qahtani lineage

name: Kumzarah
description: No reliable historical records or genealogical sources have been identified for a tribe specifically named Kumzarah in Arabian Peninsula tribal literature or GCC historical documentation. This name does not appear in established tribal genealogies or chronicles of Gulf region tribes. Without verifiable sources, no definitive information can be provided about their origins, territory, or role in Gulf history.

name: Kunud
legitimacyNotes: Claims lineage to Miqdad ibn Aswad Al Kindi, an immigrant from Yemen during the time of Prophet Muhammad. The connection to the Pre-Islamic tribal confederation that fought with Sasanian Lakhmid clients suggests historical tribal roots.
ancestorStory: The Kunud have been linked to Miqdad ibn Aswad Al Kindi, an immigrant into Oman from Yemen at the time of the Islamic prophet, Muhammad
peakPowerEra: Pre-Islamic Recent to early Islamic period
description: The Kunud (singular Al Kindi) is a tribe of the United Arab Emirates (UAE) and Oman. They trace their origin to the tribal confederation that fought with the Sasanian's Lakhmid clients in the late Pre-Islamic Recent period. By the turn of the 20th century, the Kunud, with a population of some 1,500, were mostly settled on the East Coast. They have traditionally followed the Omani Ibadi faith and have long been associated with the Sudan (Al Suwaidi).

relations.context: Long associated with the Sudan (Al Suwaidi)

name: Kuruni
legitimacyNotes: Kurdish tribal group with mixed Arabian integration
description: The Kuruni appear to be a tribal group with Kurdish origins or connections, as suggested by their alternative designation as Kurds. Historical records of Kurdish tribal elements in the Arabian Peninsula are limited and often relate to migration patterns or military service. Their specific role in GCC tribal structures and current status remains unclear from available historical sources.

name: Lakhm
legitimacyNotes: Ancient Arab tribal confederation with royal lineage, established the Lakhmid Kingdom
ancestorStory: Lakhm ibn Adi was the progenitor of the tribe that established one of the most powerful pre-Islamic Arab kingdoms. The Lakhmids ruled as vassal kings under the Sassanid Empire from their capital at Al-Hirah.
description: Lakhm was a major pre-Islamic Arab tribal confederation that established the Lakhmid Kingdom, ruling from Al-Hirah in southern Iraq from approximately 300-602 CE. They served as vassal kings under the Sassanid Empire and controlled important trade routes between Arabia and Mesopotamia. The Lakhmids were among the most powerful Arab dynasties before Islam, known for their patronage of Arabic poetry and culture. Their kingdom fell to the Sassanids in 602 CE, and the tribe was later absorbed into the broader Islamic expansion.

relations.context: Both tribes have historical presence in southern Iraq region

name: Lakhmid
legitimacyNotes: Sassanid-backed Arab client kingdom rulers of Hira
ancestorStory: The Lakhmids were descendants of Lakhm ibn Adi, establishing their kingdom at Hira under Sassanid patronage. They served as buffer rulers between the Persian Empire and Arabian tribes.
description: The Lakhmids were an Arab dynasty that ruled the kingdom of Hira in southern Iraq from the 3rd to 7th centuries CE as Sassanid clients. They controlled key trade routes and served as intermediaries between the Persian Empire and Arabian Peninsula tribes. The dynasty reached its peak under rulers like al-Mundhir III and al-Nu'man III, wielding significant influence over Arabian tribal affairs. Their kingdom was dissolved by the Sassanids in 602 CE and later conquered by Muslim Arabs during the Islamic expansion.

relations.context: They feature prominently in accounts of intertribal warfare, migrations, and alliances, including conflicts involving Rabīʿa and the Lakhmid and Ghassanid client kingdoms
both trace ancestry to Qahtanite origins in southern Iraq region

name: Lakhmids
legitimacyNotes: Pre-Islamic Arab dynasty ruling as vassals of the Sassanian Empire
ancestorStory: The Lakhmids traced their lineage to Lakham ibn Adi, establishing their dynasty in al-Hirah. They became prominent rulers under Sassanian patronage, controlling much of the Arabian Peninsula's northeastern regions.
description: The Lakhmids were a pre-Islamic Arab dynasty that ruled the kingdom of al-Hirah in southern Iraq from the 3rd to 7th centuries. They served as vassals to the Sassanian Empire, controlling trade routes and acting as a buffer against Byzantine influence and nomadic Arab tribes. The dynasty reached its peak under kings like al-Mundhir III and al-Nu'man III, extending influence across much of eastern Arabia including areas that would become part of modern Gulf states. They were conquered by the Rashidun Caliphate in 602 CE, ending their rule before the rise of the modern Gulf political structures.

relations.context: Traditionally allied with the Sasanian client state of the Lakhmids before supplanting them as rulers of al-Hirah in the 610s

name: Madhhij

description: Madhhij was one of the major ancient Qahtani tribal confederations of southern Arabia, historically based in Yemen and parts of Najran. The confederation played a significant role in pre-Islamic Arabian politics and early Islamic conquests, with various branches migrating throughout the Arabian Peninsula. Many of their sub-tribes integrated into other tribal structures over time, with some branches settling in areas that later became part of modern GCC states. While the original confederation structure no longer exists as a unified entity, genealogical connections to Madhhij remain important in Arabian tribal identity.

Al Kathiri claim descent from Madhhij confederation

name: Mahabib

description: The Mahabib are a tribal group found in the Arabian Peninsula region. They maintain traditional tribal structures and are primarily associated with nomadic pastoral activities. Limited historical documentation exists about their specific origins and major historical events. They continue to exist as an active tribal entity in the modern era.

name: Mahamid
legitimacyNotes: Claimed descent from Harb tribe through Khawlaniyah al-Qahtaniyah lineage, established political emirate in Karak region
ancestorStory: The tribe traces its origins to the Khawlaniyah al-Qahtaniyah Harb lineage
description: The Mahamid are an Arab tribe that traces its origins to the Khawlaniyah al-Qahtaniyah Harb. Originally residing in Yemen, the Hijaz between Mecca and Medina, they spread throughout the Arabian Peninsula and the Maghreb. They are known for their frequent travels, emigration and instability. When their population grew and their area of residence in Yemen, Najd and Hijaz became too narrow, they gathered to decide their future. They rejected conquering neighboring tribes (many being from Harb) and instead chose to migrate. They established the Emirate of Mahamid in the Karak region after moving through Dumat Al-Jandal and the Levant, where they became one of the largest tribes and allied with local clans like Al-Omaro Bani Uqba.

description: The Mahashir are a tribal branch with historical presence in eastern Arabia, particularly in areas that are now part of Saudi Arabia and the UAE. They were traditionally involved in pastoralism and participated in the complex tribal politics of the region. The tribe maintained connections with other Qahtani tribes and played a role in the social and economic networks of the Gulf region. They continue to exist as part of the broader tribal structure in modern Gulf states.

peakPowerEra: early Islamic period through Mahra Sultanate (lasting five centuries until 1967)
description: The Mahris are an Arab ethnographic group primarily inhabiting South Arabia, especially in the Al-Mahra Governorate in Yemen and the island of Socotra. They are descended from Mahra bin Haydan and are known for their unique Mahri language, a Modern South Arabian language. They played significant roles in early Islamic conquests of North Africa and Spain, and maintained the Mahra Sultanate for five centuries until 1967. They are famous for their Mahri camels and have distinctive genetic markers showing minimal admixture with >85.9% Arabian ancestry.

relations.context: Mahri lineage traces back through Quda'a confederation

name: Makatim
legitimacyNotes: Traditional merchant family authority through trade networks and commercial influence
description: The Makatim are a prominent merchant family historically based in Kuwait and other Gulf ports. They were involved in the traditional Gulf economy of pearling, trade, and maritime commerce. The family established trading networks across the Arabian Gulf and Indian Ocean routes. They remain active in modern Gulf business communities, particularly in Kuwait.

name: Manai'i

ancestorStory: The tribe traces its lineage to an ancestor named Manee, from whom they derive their tribal name. They are considered part of the broader Qahtani tribal confederation of southern Arabian origin.
description: The Manai'i are a Qahtani tribe historically based in Qatar and parts of the eastern Arabian Peninsula. They have been involved in traditional Gulf activities including pearling, fishing, and maritime trade. The tribe has maintained its presence in modern Qatar where members continue to play roles in contemporary society. They have historical trade partnerships with other tribes including the Dahaminah.

name: Manasir
legitimacyNotes: Critical research suggests that the current Manasir community should be viewed as a voluntary amalgamation throughout the centuries between indigenous mostly Nubian groups, descendants of emigrating Arabs and recruited tribal minorities living among them or in the adjoining areas. By recognizing a common genealogical pedigree, all members establish a system of mutual respect, rights and obligations.
ancestorStory: According to the current oral tradition of many Manasir, their ancestor is called Mansur and belongs to the line of descendants of al-'Abbas, the uncle of Muhammad. Multiple explanations are offered: some claim their apical forefather is Mansur bin Qahtan belonging to the Southern Arabs called Qahtaniin; others trace their origin to the Kawahla saying they are sons of Mansur bin 'Aim; still others insist they are descendants of cousins from a Shaiqi clan called al-Hankab who migrated after internal fights, with Mansur being a direct descendant of King Sabir.
traditionalEconomy: The riverain Manasir pursue small scale agriculture on alluvial soils in the immediate vicinity of the Nile. Their most important cash crop is a wide variety of dates. They are also renowned for their skill in building mud houses and used to float wood from the region of Atbarah. The Bedouin portion herds goats, sheep and camels in desert valleys.
description: The Manasir constitute one of many Sunni Arab riverine tribes of Northern Sudan. They inhabit the region of the Fourth Cataract of the Nile and call their homeland Dar al-Manasir. Similar to their neighbouring tribes, the upstream Rubatab and the downstream Shaiqiyah, the Manasir are an indigenous Nile culture who adopted Islam and speak Arabic as their first language. Unlike other riverine tribes of Sudan, a considerable part of their population lives as Bedouins in the adjacent Bayuda Desert. The nomadic life of herding their stock of goats, sheep and camels in desert valleys is however limited for many to the rainy season, coinciding with the annual inundation of the Nile.

description: The Manasir al-Badiyah are the Bedouin branch of the larger Manasir tribal confederation, traditionally based in the desert regions between Abu Dhabi and Oman. They maintained nomadic pastoralist traditions longer than their settled relatives, specializing in camel herding and desert trade routes. The tribe has historical presence in the Liwa oasis region and the Empty Quarter, with traditional grazing rights across tribal territories. Today they are integrated into UAE society while maintaining cultural traditions and tribal identity.

description: The Manasir al-Nil are a branch of the broader Manasir tribal confederation that settled along the Nile River in Sudan. They represent a Sudanese riverain community that maintains genealogical connections to the Arabian Peninsula Manasir tribes. While geographically distant from the GCC, they share common ancestry with UAE-based Manasir tribes and maintain cultural and lineage ties to the broader Manasir tribal network.

name: Maraziq

description: The Maraziq (singular Al Marzooqi or Al Marzouqi) is a tribe of the United Arab Emirates, traditionally associated with the settlement of Rams, north of Ras Al Khaimah. The tribe settled the western area of Rams, specifically the Bani Humud neighbourhood, between Rams and Hulaylah. They lived in traditional khaimahs - small stone-walled houses with gabled barasti (palm-frond) roofs. The Maraziq population left the area by the early 1950s and are now dispersed throughout the UAE.

name: Mawali
legitimacyNotes: Islamic legal status as non-Arab Muslims with tribal protection
description: Mawali refers to non-Arab Muslims who converted to Islam and became clients of Arab tribes, particularly during the early Islamic expansion period. In the Arabian Peninsula and Gulf region, they included Persians, other non-Arab peoples, and freed slaves who gained protection and social status through tribal affiliation. They played important roles in early Islamic administration, scholarship, and commerce. As a social category rather than a tribe, the mawali system largely dissolved over time as Islamic society evolved and tribal distinctions became less rigid.

name: Mazari
legitimacyNotes: The name 'Mazari' appears to be used by multiple unrelated tribal groups, suggesting it may be a toponymic designation rather than indicating true blood kinship between these groups.
description: The Mazari represent multiple distinct tribal groups rather than a single unified tribe. The text identifies at least two separate Mazari tribes: an Emirati Bedouin tribe of the United Arab Emirates and a Baloch tribe settled in Rajanpur and Sindh, Pakistan. These appear to be separate entities that share only the name 'Mazari', likely derived from different etymological origins.

name: Mazari (Baloch)
legitimacyNotes: Baloch tribal identity with traditional leadership structure
ancestorStory: The Mazari trace their lineage to Mazar, a prominent figure among the Baloch tribes. They are part of the larger Baloch tribal confederation that migrated from Balochistan to the Arabian Peninsula.
description: The Mazari are a Baloch tribal group that forms part of the broader Baloch diaspora in the Arabian Peninsula. Historically, they migrated from the Balochistan region and settled in various parts of the Gulf states, particularly maintaining their distinct Baloch cultural identity. Like other Baloch tribes in the region, they have traditionally been involved in pastoralism and have integrated into the social fabric of Gulf societies while preserving their tribal structure. They remain an active community within the larger Baloch population in the GCC states.

name: Mazari (Emirati)
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal authority based on genealogical claims and settlement patterns in UAE territories
description: The Mazari are a tribal group found within the United Arab Emirates, representing one of the smaller tribal entities in the federation. They are primarily settled in specific regions of the UAE and maintain traditional tribal social structures. Like many UAE tribes, they have adapted to the modern state system while preserving their tribal identity and customs.

name: Mazrui
description: The Mazrui or Mazar'i were an Omani Arab clan that reigned over some areas of East Africa, especially Kenya, from the 18th to the 20th century. In the 18th century they governed Mombasa and other coastal places and opposed the Omani Al Bu Sa'id Dynasty that ruled over Zanzibar. They were one of the groups that most actively resisted British rule when the British East Africa Protectorate was established in the late 19th century, along with the Kikuyu and Kamba people.

relations.context: Opposed the Omani Al Bu Sa'id Dynasty that ruled over Zanzibar in the 18th century

name: Misaid
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal lineage and local consensus
description: Al Misaid is a tribal group found in the Arabian Peninsula with presence in Gulf regions. Limited historical documentation exists about their specific origins and traditional territories. They maintain tribal identity and kinship structures typical of Arabian Peninsula tribes. Their current status and activities within the GCC context require further historical verification.

name: Miyadilah
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal authority through lineage and pastoral leadership
description: The Miyadilah are a small Arabian tribal group with limited documented historical presence in the Gulf region. Their exact origins, territorial base, and historical significance remain poorly documented in major tribal genealogical sources. They appear to have been associated with pastoral activities in the Arabian Peninsula but lack prominent historical records of major political or economic influence in the GCC states.

name: Mizahamiyin
ancestorStory: Descended from Muzahim, though specific genealogical details vary in tribal accounts.
description: The Mizahamiyin are a tribal group whose historical presence and exact lineage remain poorly documented in available sources. They appear to be connected to the broader tribal networks of the Arabian Peninsula, though their specific role in Gulf politics and economy is not well established in mainstream historical accounts. Their current status and territorial associations require further genealogical research to establish definitively.

name: Mudar
legitimacyNotes: Modern scholarship treats these genealogies with caution, noting that they reflect later attempts to systematize tribal relations and political rivalries rather than straightforward history. The confederation included diverse tribes with varying lifestyles and political orientations grouped together under the collective name 'Mudar'.
ancestorName: Mudar b. Nizār b. Maʿadd b. ʿAdnān
ancestorStory: Genealogists trace Mudar's descent from Mudar b. Nizār b. Maʿadd b. ʿAdnān, placing him among the foundational ancestors of the so-called 'northern Arabs'. Mudar is said to have had two sons, from whom the major branches of the confederation descended. Genealogical narratives also preserve legendary elements, such as the division of Nizār's inheritance and symbolic associations.
traditionalEconomy: Mixed - ranging from sedentary groups associated with sanctuary towns to highly mobile pastoral nomads
description: Mudar was one of the principal tribal confederations of northern and central Arabia in late antiquity and the early Islamic period. Rising to power over the Hejaz in the fifth century after supplanting the Banu Ghassan, Mudar occupied large areas of the Hejaz, Tihamah, Najd, and adjoining regions. Together with Rabīʿa, it formed one of the two largest groupings of tribes tracing descent from Nizar ibn Maʿadd. In later historiography, 'Mudar' came to designate not only a genealogical lineage but also a broad political and cultural bloc opposed to the southern (Yemeni) tribal groupings.

subTribes.name: Qays Aylan
subTribes.description: Key Adnanite branch

subTribes.name: Quraysh
subTribes.legitimacyNotes: True blood lineage traced to Fihr ibn Malik of the Kinana tribe, with clear genealogical connections to Adnan. The tribal name either derives from 'taqarrush' meaning 'coming together' or from their ancestor Qusayy ibn Kilab who unified the scattered descendants.
subTribes.ancestorStory: Fihr ibn Malik was the progenitor of the Quraysh, descended from Adnan the Ishmaelite. He led the warriors of Kinana and Khuzayma in defense of the Kaaba against tribes from Yemen. His descendants lived in scattered, nomadic groups among their Kinana relatives until Qusayy ibn Kilab, a sixth-generation descendant, unified them and established Quraysh control over Mecca.
subTribes.traditionalEconomy: Trade caravans, pilgrimage services, mining, taxes from pilgrims
subTribes.description: The Quraysh are an Arab tribe who controlled Mecca before the rise of Islam and became wealthy merchants dominating trade between the Indian Ocean, East Africa, and the Mediterranean. They were divided into ten main clans including the Banu Hashim, into which Prophet Muhammad was born. Initially opposed to Islam, they eventually converted after Muhammad's conquest of Mecca in 630 CE. After Muhammad's death, leadership of the Muslim community traditionally passed to members of the Quraysh.

relations.context: Together with Rabīʿa, it formed one of the two largest groupings of tribes tracing descent from Nizar ibn Maʿadd
In the pre-Islamic era, Mudar was usually politically dominated by Himyar, until the collapse of the latter
Mudar rose to power over the Hejaz in the fifth century, after supplanting its predecessor, the Banu Ghassan
They feature prominently in accounts of intertribal warfare, migrations, and alliances, including conflicts involving Rabīʿa and the Lakhmid and Ghassanid client kingdoms
They feature prominently in accounts of intertribal warfare, migrations, and alliances, including conflicts involving Rabīʿa and the Lakhmid and Ghassanid client kingdoms
Sub-tribe of the Mudar tribal confederation, specifically of the Amir ibn Sa'sa'a branch

name: Mudhar
legitimacyNotes: Descended from Mudhar ibn Nizar, one of the primary divisions of Adnanite Arabs
ancestorStory: Mudhar was one of the sons of Nizar ibn Ma'add ibn Adnan, representing one of the major tribal confederations of northern Arabia. The Mudharis became one of the two main divisions of Adnanite tribes.
description: Mudhar was one of the great tribal confederations of pre-Islamic and early Islamic Arabia, comprising numerous tribes descended from Mudhar ibn Nizar. They were primarily based in the Najd and northern Arabian Peninsula, representing the northern Adnanite Arab division in opposition to the Rabiah confederation. The Mudhari tribes included major groups like Quraish, Tamim, and Qays, who played crucial roles in early Islamic conquests and later Arab expansion. While the confederation as a political entity is historical, many of its constituent tribes remain active throughout the Arabian Peninsula and Gulf region.

relations.context: The two main branches of North Arabian (Adnanite) tribes
Mudhar descended from Adnan through Nizar

name: al-Muleikab
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal authority based on ancestral lineage and established settlement patterns
description: Al-Muleikab is a tribal group found in the Arabian Peninsula. Limited historical documentation makes it difficult to establish their specific origins, traditional territories, or detailed genealogical connections. They appear to maintain their tribal identity in the modern era, though their current distribution and activities require further research to establish definitively.

name: Mutair (Ikhwan forces)
legitimacyNotes: Part of the Ikhwan religious-military movement under Ibn Saud's leadership
ancestorStory: Descends from Mutayr ibn Ghutayf of the Ghatafan tribal confederation. The tribe traces its lineage through the Hawazin branch of Qays Aylan.
description: Mutair is a major Najdi Bedouin tribe that became one of the most prominent members of the Ikhwan movement in early 20th century Saudi Arabia. Historically based in central Najd, they played a crucial role in Ibn Saud's military campaigns to unify Saudi Arabia between 1910-1930. The tribe later rebelled against Ibn Saud in the famous Ikhwan Revolt (1927-1930) before being subdued. Today, Mutair remains one of Saudi Arabia's largest and most influential tribes, with members across the Gulf states.

relations.context: Battle of Qaysar 1924
Initially allied in Ikhwan movement, later rebelled against Saudi rule
Fellow Ikhwan tribes who participated in the revolt together
Traditional Najdi tribal competition over grazing rights and territory

name: Mutayr
legitimacyNotes: The tribal lineage appears to be authentic blood lineage supported by DNA testing showing shared haplogroups and common ancestry among members. The tribe has embraced genetic genealogy to study their genealogy using modern technologies.
ancestorStory: According to Ahmad al-Qalqashandi (d. 1418), the Mutayr tribe belongs to Ghatafan who are descendants of Ishmael son of Abraham. DNA tests have confirmed that male participants from Mutayr share the same haplogroup and common ancestor, with most Y-Chromosome lineage from the J1 Haplogroup network.
description: Mutayr is a large Arab tribe originating from the northern Hejaz near Medina, whose territory extends from the Hijaz to Najd and then to Kuwait. The tribe underwent a major eastward migration in the 17th century into central Arabia, becoming the dominant nomadic tribe of Al-Qaseem. They played a crucial role in early 20th century Arabian history through their involvement in the Ikhwan movement under Faisal Al-Dewish. Originally Bedouin, the tribe has now largely settled in cities and towns across Saudi Arabia, establishing up to 400 villages across the country.

relations.context: Mutayr aided by Qahtan fought against Anazzah in 18th century, later superseded by Otaibah
A rivalry developed between Mutayr and Utaybah, who inhabited roughly the same areas as Mutayr in central Arabia after both tribes migrated from Hejaz.
Mutayr displaced Harb during their eastward migration, forcing Harb to move northwards.
Mutayr displaced Anizzah during their eastward migration, forcing Anizzah to move northwards.
Led alliance against Bani Khalid in decisive 1823 battle
Allied with Ajman in Ikhwan rebellion against Ibn Saud

name: Al-'Ulwa
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal authority through lineage connection to Mutayr confederation
description: Al-'Ulwa is a major branch of the Mutayr tribal confederation, one of the largest Bedouin tribes of the Arabian Peninsula. They are traditionally based in the Najd region of central Saudi Arabia and have historically been involved in nomadic pastoralism and tribal warfare. The tribe played significant roles in various historical conflicts and migrations throughout the Arabian Peninsula. Today they remain an active tribal group within Saudi Arabia with members also found in other Gulf states.

relations.context: Historical alliance during Saudi state formation

name: Bani Abdullah
legitimacyNotes: Branch of the larger Mutayr confederation with traditional authority through lineage and pastoral territories
ancestorStory: Descended from Abdullah, an ancestor within the Mutayr tribal confederation. The branch maintains its distinct identity while being part of the larger Mutayr structure.
description: Bani Abdullah is a prominent branch of the Mutayr tribal confederation, one of the major Bedouin tribes of the Arabian Peninsula. They are traditionally based in Najd and have historically practiced nomadic pastoralism across central and eastern Arabia. As part of Mutayr, they participated in the major tribal movements and conflicts of the 18th and 19th centuries, including interactions with the Saudi state. Today they maintain their tribal identity while many members have settled in modern GCC states, particularly Saudi Arabia and Kuwait.

relations.context: Both tribes practiced nomadic pastoralism across similar territories in eastern Arabia
Historical cooperation as major Bedouin tribes in the Arabian Peninsula

name: Braih
legitimacyNotes: Recognized sub-tribe within the Mutayr confederation with traditional grazing and territorial rights
ancestorStory: Braih is considered one of the founding branches of the Mutayr tribal confederation. The lineage traces back to the early divisions within Mutayr that established the major sub-tribal units.
description: Braih is a sub-tribe of the larger Mutayr confederation, one of the major Bedouin tribal groups of central Arabia. Historically based in the Najd region of Saudi Arabia, they participated in the traditional nomadic pastoral economy and tribal raids. As part of Mutayr, they played a role in the early Saudi state formations and the unification of Saudi Arabia. The tribe remains active today with members settled across Saudi Arabia and other Gulf states.

relations.context: Historical alliance as part of Mutayr's support for Saudi unification

name: Nahood
legitimacyNotes: Traditional Bedouin tribal authority based on lineage and historical presence in Arabian Peninsula
description: Al-Nahood is a tribal name mentioned in Arabian Peninsula genealogical records, though detailed historical documentation is limited. Like many smaller Arabian tribes, they were traditionally engaged in nomadic pastoralism across the desert regions. Their current status and specific historical role in Gulf region politics remains unclear due to limited documented presence in major historical events.

name: Na'im
legitimacyNotes: Established blood lineage tribe with historical territorial control and recognized sub-tribal structure
description: The Na'im is a traditionally Ghafiri Arab tribe prominent in the United Arab Emirates and other Gulf countries. Historically one of the most significant tribes in the hinterland, they were widely distributed across Trucial Oman and the Sultanate of Oman. At their peak in the early 19th century, they numbered around 20,000 men in Buraimi and were arguably the dominant force west of the Hajar Mountains. The tribe's traditional heartland was the Buraimi oasis and nearby Al Ain, where they expanded at the expense of the Dhawahir tribe. Despite their historical prominence, the tribe experienced decline due to persistent internal divisions among sub-tribes, with their fighting strength dropping from 2,000 men in the early 20th century to just 300-400 rifles by the 1940s.

subTribes.name: Khawatir
subTribes.legitimacyNotes: The Khawatir are described as a legitimate subsection of the Na'im tribe, indicating a genuine blood lineage relationship rather than a political confederation.
subTribes.ancestorStory: <UNKNOWN>
subTribes.traditionalEconomy: Bedouin pastoralism with camels, sheep, goats, cattle, and date palm cultivation
subTribes.description: The Khawatir is an Arab tribe of the United Arab Emirates, forming one of three subsections of the Na'im tribe. A Bedouin tribe whose traditional territory stretched from Buraimi north to the Jiri Plain, they mostly settled around the Buraimi oasis. They adopted Wahhabism in the 19th century in response to encroachment by the Al Bu Falah and their Manasir allies. The tribe frequently came into conflict with the Sharqiyin of Fujairah but would ally with them against the powerful Qawasim of Sharjah and Ras Al Khaimah. They also settled in Dhaid in Sharjah and were involved in various political disputes and alliances throughout their history.

subTribes.name: Al Bu Shamis
subTribes.legitimacyNotes: Originally one of three sections of the larger Na'im tribal confederation, the Al Bu Shamis evolved from being part of a larger tribal structure to becoming virtually independent, particularly through close association with the Al Bu Falah ruling family of Abu Dhabi
subTribes.ancestorStory: <UNKNOWN>
subTribes.traditionalEconomy: Bedouin nomadism, later settled agriculture in oases and strategic coastal settlements
subTribes.description: The Al Bu Shamis is an Arab tribe of the United Arab Emirates, also evident in northern Oman. Originally one of three sections of the Na'im confederation (along with Al Bu Kharaiban and Khawatir), the Al Bu Shamis has become virtually independent and closely associated with the ruling Al Bu Falah of Abu Dhabi. Originally forming the Bedouin component of the Na'im, they retained a predominantly nomadic character into the 1950s with approximately 1,000 individuals including around 400 armed men. They later settled strategically significant oases and coastal settlements, becoming headmen in places like Hamriyah and Al Heera.

relations.context: Despite their alliance under the Bani Yas, the Bani Kaab were rivals to the Na'im and had pushed sections of the Na'im north as far as the Jiri plain. In the 1940s they came together when oil concessions loomed.
Conflict over Wadi Hatta in 1905, where Bani Qitab built a fort and harassed caravans, threatening the Na'im who were traditionally from Buraimi

name: Naqbiyin
legitimacyNotes: Appears to be a genuine tribal lineage with clear territorial associations and historical presence in the region dating back at least 300 years in some locations.
description: The Naqbiyin is a tribe of the United Arab Emirates that has been influential in the tribal politics of Sharjah and Ras Al Khaimah emirates. By the early 20th century, they had settled throughout the region including Khor Fakkan, Kalba, Dibba, and Fahlain, with a population of approximately 1,800 people primarily engaged in agriculture. They are known for their strategic settlements and close relationship with the Qawasim rulers.

relations.context: Habus gradually bought up Naqbiyin territory in Wadi Naqab after the Naqbiyin fell foul of local rulers around 1695-1740

name: Nawaljiyin
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal leadership through established family lines
description: The Nawaljiyin are a tribal group mentioned in Arabian Peninsula genealogical records, though detailed historical documentation about their specific origins, territorial base, and role in Gulf history remains limited in widely available sources. Further research in local archives and oral traditions would be needed to establish their precise historical significance and current status.

name: Northern Bani Qitab
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal lineage authority through Qitab ancestry
ancestorStory: Northern branch descended from Qitab, distinguished from southern Bani Qitab settlements through geographic and political divisions.
description: Northern Bani Qitab represents the northern territorial branch of the broader Bani Qitab tribal confederation in the Arabian Peninsula. They were traditionally distinguished from their southern counterparts through geographic settlement patterns and pastoral territories. The tribe maintained traditional Bedouin lifestyle through camel and sheep herding across northern Arabian territories. Their historical significance lies primarily in their role within the broader Qitab tribal structure rather than as independent political actors.

relations.context: Both tribes shared northern Arabian pastoral territories

name: Obaidli
legitimacyNotes: Established merchant and pearling family with traditional leadership in coastal communities
ancestorStory: The tribe traces its lineage to Obaid Allah, an ancestor who settled in the Gulf coast region. The name reflects their descent from this progenitor.
description: The Obaidli are a tribal group historically present in the Gulf coastal regions, particularly in areas that are now part of the UAE and Qatar. They were traditionally involved in pearling, fishing, and coastal trade activities. The tribe has maintained its presence in Gulf society and continues to be recognized in the modern GCC states. They represent one of the smaller but established tribal entities in the region's complex social fabric.

relations.context: Both tribes settled in similar Gulf coastal areas

name: Otaibah
legitimacyNotes: The tribe claims descent from pre-Islamic Hawazin through documented genealogical records. While most genealogy is based on oral tradition dating to the Middle Ages and earlier, there is scholarly verification through historical manuscripts including Hisham ibn al-Kalbi's 8th-century work 'The Great Ancestry'.
ancestorName: Otaibah ibn Guzayah ibn Jusham ibn Sa'd ibn Bakr ibn Hawazin
ancestorStory: According to the 8th-century manuscript by Hisham ibn al-Kalbi, 'Otaibah' is attributed to a standard or banner belonging to the tribe of Hawazin. The name derives from Otaibah Ibn Guzayah Ibn Jusham Ibn Sa'd ibn Bakr Ibn Hawazin. After most Hawazin clans departed from Hejaz and Najd, those who remained formed the largest Hawazin alliance and were named Otaibah.
description: The Otaibah is one of the biggest Arabian tribes originating in the Arabian Peninsula, with distribution spanning throughout Saudi Arabia, especially in Najd and Hejaz, and the Middle East. They are descended from the Bedouin and trace back to the Mudar family, belonging to the Qays ʿAylān confederacy through its previous name, Hawazin. The tribe remained as the largest Hawazin alliance after most other Hawazin clans departed from Hejaz and Najd to other lands including the Levant, Iraq, Egypt, North Africa, and Persia.

relations.context: Otaibah is descended from and represents the largest remaining alliance of the Hawazin confederation
Conflicts over pastures in central Najd during 18th century, with Otaibah eventually superseding Anazzah
Mutayr aided by Qahtan fought against Anazzah in 18th century, later superseded by Otaibah

name: Qahtan (Qahtanite lineage)
legitimacyNotes: Genealogical legitimacy as descendants of Qahtan, the legendary ancestor of southern Arabian tribes
ancestorStory: Identified by some with the biblical Joktan
description: Southern Arabs / Pure Arabs / al-Arab al-Ariba. Origin: South Arabia (Yemen, Hadhramaut).

subTribes.name: Himyar
subTribes.legitimacyNotes: Originally a confederation of tribes that formed a unified kingdom; ruled by kings from the dhū-Raydān tribe after independence from Qataban
subTribes.traditionalEconomy: international trade in frankincense, myrrh, and ivory; hub linking Mediterranean, Middle East, India, and East Africa
subTribes.description: Himyar was a powerful ancient kingdom and polity in the southern highlands of Yemen that existed from 110 BCE until the early 6th century CE. Initially integrated into the Qatabanian kingdom until 110 BCE, it became an independent kingdom ruled by kings from the dhū-Raydān tribe. The kingdom was a confederation of tribes that created a wealthy, sophisticated society with rich trade networks linking the Mediterranean, Middle East, India, and East Africa. Himyar conquered neighboring kingdoms including Saba', Qataban, and Haḍramaut, uniting southern Arabia for the first time by 300 CE. The kingdom underwent significant religious transformations, converting from South Arabian polytheism to Judaism in the 4th century under Malkikarib Yuhamin, and later to Christianity after the Aksumite conquest in 530 CE.

subTribes.name: Kahlan
subTribes.legitimacyNotes: A major tribal confederation of ancient Saba', with genuine historical presence in Yemen before forced migration due to Himyarite pressure
subTribes.ancestorName: Kahlan bin Saba bin Yishjab bin Yarub bin Qahtan
subTribes.ancestorStory: Descended from Kahlan bin Saba bin Yishjab bin Yarub bin Qahtan, making them one of the main Qahtani confederations originating from ancient Saba' kingdom in Yemen.
subTribes.peakPowerEra: Ancient Sabaean period, before 2nd century BC
subTribes.traditionalEconomy: Highland pastoralism and agriculture in Yemen, later nomadic pastoralism during migrations
subTribes.description: Kahlan was one of the main tribal confederations of Saba' in Ancient Yemen, descended from Kahlan bin Saba bin Yishjab bin Yarub bin Qahtan. Originally dominant in the Yemeni highlands, they were forced out by Himyar in the 2nd century BC and migrated northwards throughout Arabia after the destruction of the Marib Dam in the 3rd century AD, eventually reaching as far as Mesopotamia and Syria.

subTribes.name: Qahtan (tribe)
subTribes.legitimacyNotes: This is a major tribal confederation with documented historical presence across the Arabian Peninsula. The confederation structure with three main constituent tribes (Sanhan, Junb, Rufaida) suggests a political alliance rather than pure blood lineage.
subTribes.description: Qahtān is an Arab tribal confederation composed of three main tribes: Sanhan, Junb, and Rufaida. Today, members of the tribe and its sub-tribes are based across the Arabian Peninsula including Yemen, Saudi Arabia, Oman, Qatar, Iraq, Kuwait, and the United Arab Emirates. The tribe enjoyed prominence in the Najd region, particularly in Al-Quwai'iyah, and expanded its influence in southern Najd before later returning to its original homeland in the south.

relations.context: Direct descendant tribe maintaining the Qahtanite name and identity
Claims Qahtanite descent through the Saba lineage
Traditional genealogical division between Qahtanite (southern) and Adnanite (northern) Arab lineages

name: Qahtan (tribe)
legitimacyNotes: This is a major tribal confederation with documented historical presence across the Arabian Peninsula. The confederation structure with three main constituent tribes (Sanhan, Junb, Rufaida) suggests a political alliance rather than pure blood lineage.
description: Qahtān is an Arab tribal confederation composed of three main tribes: Sanhan, Junb, and Rufaida. Today, members of the tribe and its sub-tribes are based across the Arabian Peninsula including Yemen, Saudi Arabia, Oman, Qatar, Iraq, Kuwait, and the United Arab Emirates. The tribe enjoyed prominence in the Najd region, particularly in Al-Quwai'iyah, and expanded its influence in southern Najd before later returning to its original homeland in the south.

subTribes.name: Bani Hajer
subTribes.legitimacyNotes: Claimed Qahtanite lineage through Abida tribe, with documented leadership succession and migration patterns
subTribes.ancestorStory: Al Hawajer are a Qahtanite tribe from Abida, they resided near Sarat Abida also known as 'alhirja' near Thahran Al Janoub
subTribes.description: Bani Hajer, also known as al-Hawajir, are an influential Qahtanite tribe in the Arabian Peninsula. Originally from Abida near Sarat Abida (also known as 'alhirja' near Thahran Al Janoub), they are known for their trade activities and bringing foreign goods to Arabia. They migrated to Lower Najd in the early 13th century under Sheikh Mohammed Bin Shaba'an, then later to Al Ahsa'a. They are considered nomadic Bedouins, though many have settled in cities and hold government offices. They were honored by King Abdulaziz Al Saud with the title 'Khaznat Thafar' (Vault of Victory) for their crucial role in the Battle of Kinzan against Al Ajman in 1914-1915.

name: Qataban
legitimacyNotes: Ancient South Arabian kingdom and tribal confederation based on control of frankincense trade routes
ancestorStory: Qataban was one of the four major kingdoms of ancient South Arabia, tracing lineage to Qahtanite origins. The tribe established a powerful kingdom controlling crucial incense trade routes between the 8th century BCE and 3rd century CE.
description: Qataban was one of the four major ancient South Arabian kingdoms, controlling territory in what is now southern Yemen and parts of modern-day Saudi Arabia. They dominated the frankincense trade routes from the 8th century BCE to 3rd century CE, with their capital at Timna. The kingdom declined due to changing trade patterns and pressure from Himyarite expansion. While historically significant to Arabian Peninsula tribal genealogy, they have limited direct relevance to modern GCC tribal structures, existing primarily as an ancestral reference point for various Qahtanite lineages.

relations.context: Initially integrated into Qatabanian kingdom until 110 BCE; later conquered Qataban in c. 200 CE
Qataban represents one of the major ancient Qahtanite tribal kingdoms

name: Qawasem
legitimacyNotes: Maritime power and traditional ruling family with religious scholarship heritage
ancestorStory: The Al Qasimi trace their lineage to Qasim ibn Raziq, who established the family's presence in the region. They became prominent through their maritime activities and religious scholarship in the Gulf.
description: The Al Qasimi (Qawasem) are a prominent Arabian dynasty that has ruled Sharjah and Ras Al Khaimah in the UAE since the 18th century. Historically based along the Trucial Coast, they were major maritime powers controlling key trade routes in the Persian Gulf and engaging in both commerce and naval warfare. The British initially viewed them as pirates, leading to several military campaigns, but they later became key allies through the Trucial States system. Today they remain the ruling families of Sharjah and Ras Al Khaimah emirates.

relations.context: Same tribal confederation, Al Qasimi is modern designation
Historical competition for territorial control in Trucial States
Modern UAE federation partners as ruling families

name: Qays Aylan
legitimacyNotes: Major Adnanite lineage branch with extensive sub-tribal divisions
ancestorStory: Qays ibn Aylan was a major Adnanite ancestor whose descendants formed numerous influential tribes. His lineage produced many prominent Arabian tribes that spread across the peninsula.
description: Key Adnanite branch

relations.context: Direct descent from Adnanite lineage

name: Qubaisat
legitimacyNotes: Established sub-tribe of the larger Bani Yas confederation
ancestorStory: <UNKNOWN>

name: Qubaisi
description: Al Qubaisi (Al Qubaisiat) are a sub-tribe of Bani Yas, of Abu Dhabi, closely associated with the Bani Yas of Abu Dhabi. The Al Qubaisi family shares close relations with the leading royal family of the United Arab Emirates through Salama bint Butti Al Qubaisi, mother of Sheikh Zayed bin Sultan Al Nahyan, the founder of the UAE. As a subset of the Bani Yas tribe, the Al Qubaisi family has been influential in various aspects of Emirati society, including politics, business, and community affairs. They have played roles in the development and progress of the UAE, contributing to various sectors and institutions within the country.

name: Quraysh
legitimacyNotes: True blood lineage traced to Fihr ibn Malik of the Kinana tribe, with clear genealogical connections to Adnan. The tribal name either derives from 'taqarrush' meaning 'coming together' or from their ancestor Qusayy ibn Kilab who unified the scattered descendants.
ancestorStory: Fihr ibn Malik was the progenitor of the Quraysh, descended from Adnan the Ishmaelite. He led the warriors of Kinana and Khuzayma in defense of the Kaaba against tribes from Yemen. His descendants lived in scattered, nomadic groups among their Kinana relatives until Qusayy ibn Kilab, a sixth-generation descendant, unified them and established Quraysh control over Mecca.
traditionalEconomy: Trade caravans, pilgrimage services, mining, taxes from pilgrims
description: The Quraysh are an Arab tribe who controlled Mecca before the rise of Islam and became wealthy merchants dominating trade between the Indian Ocean, East Africa, and the Mediterranean. They were divided into ten main clans including the Banu Hashim, into which Prophet Muhammad was born. Initially opposed to Islam, they eventually converted after Muhammad's conquest of Mecca in 630 CE. After Muhammad's death, leadership of the Muslim community traditionally passed to members of the Quraysh.

name: Qurra
legitimacyNotes: Branch of the larger Hilal confederation with recognized lineage
ancestorStory: Qurra was a son of Hilal ibn Amir, from whom this branch of the Banu Hilal takes its name. The tribe migrated westward with other Hilali branches during the great Arab migrations.
description: The Qurra were a branch of the Banu Hilal confederation, descended from Hilal ibn Amir of the Hawazin. They participated in the great Hilali migrations westward across North Africa in the 10th-11th centuries. As part of the larger Hilal tribal movement, they were primarily Bedouin pastoralists who played a significant role in the Arabization of the Maghreb. Their direct presence in the modern Gulf region is limited, as most Qurra branches settled in western territories during the medieval migrations.

name: Rabīʿa
legitimacyNotes: Claimed descent from Rabīʿa ibn Nizār, major Adnanite tribal confederation
ancestorStory: Rabīʿa was one of the sons of Nizār ibn Maʿadd ibn Adnān, representing one of the major divisions of northern Arabian tribes. The Rabīʿa confederation emerged from his lineage and became one of the most significant tribal groupings in pre-Islamic Arabia.
description: Rabīʿa was one of the major Adnanite tribal confederations of pre-Islamic Arabia, claiming descent from Rabīʿa ibn Nizār. The confederation included prominent tribes such as Bakr ibn Wāʾil and ʿAbd al-Qays, and controlled territories across northeastern Arabia and parts of Iraq. They were known for their conflicts with their Mudari cousins and played significant roles in pre-Islamic tribal politics and early Islamic conquests. While the confederation as a unified entity is historical, many of its constituent tribal branches continue to exist across the Arabian Peninsula and Gulf region today.

description: Rabi'a was one of the major ancient Arabian tribal confederations, descended from Rabi'a ibn Nizar ibn Ma'ad ibn Adnan. They were prominent in northern and eastern Arabia during the pre-Islamic and early Islamic periods. The confederation included several important sub-tribes that played significant roles in Arabian history. Many of their branches later settled in various parts of the Arabian Peninsula, including areas that are now part of the GCC states, though the original confederation structure dissolved over time.

name: Rabi'ah
legitimacyNotes: Established blood lineage recognized by classical Arab genealogists as one of the two main Adnanite branches
ancestorStory: Rabi'a ibn Nizar is considered the patriarch of one of the two main branches of the North Arabian (Adnanite) tribes, representing a major division in Arabian tribal genealogy.

description: Rabi'a ibn Nizar is the patriarch of one of two main branches of the 'North Arabian' (Adnanite) tribes, the other being founded by Mudhar. The tribe has numerous important branches including Abd al-Qays, Anizah, Banu Bakr, Taghlib, and others. Originally from the Tihamah region of western Arabia, they migrated northwards and eastwards, with different branches settling across the Arabian Peninsula, Mesopotamia, and eventually Egypt. Many were Christian before Islam, and the tribe produced numerous notable figures throughout Islamic history.

subTribes.name: Banu Hanifa
subTribes.legitimacyNotes: Classical Arab genealogists counted them as a Christian branch of Bani Bakr, but they led an independent existence prior to Islam. Later assimilated into Anizah tribe.
subTribes.description: Banu Hanifa is an ancient Arab tribe that inhabited the area of al-Yamama in central Saudi Arabia. Members of the great Rabi'ah branch of North Arabian tribes, they were primarily sedentary farmers living in settlements along the wadis of eastern Nejd. The tribe played a significant role in early Islamic history, initially converting to Islam but then apostatizing under their own prophet Musaylima, leading to the Ridda Wars. After their defeat, they rejoined the Islamic state and later spread to various regions including Iraq and Egypt. The ruling House of Saud of Saudi Arabia are Hanifites, and according to King Salman Al Saud, the tribe eventually assimilated into the larger Anizah tribe.

The family split from their Sharjah cousins in the 19th century, establishing separate emirates. Today they remain the hereditary rulers of Ras Al Khaimah emirate as part of the United Arab Emirates federation.

description: Saba was an ancient Arabian kingdom and dynasty based in Yemen, famous for the legendary Queen of Sheba mentioned in biblical and Quranic accounts. The Sabaean kingdom controlled important trade routes between Africa, Arabia, and the Mediterranean, accumulating wealth through incense and spice trade. Their capital was at Marib, known for its great dam and advanced irrigation systems. While historically centered in Yemen, Sabaean influence extended throughout southern Arabia and their genealogical legacy is claimed by various Qahtani tribes across the Arabian Peninsula.

They established one of the most powerful kingdoms in pre-Islamic Arabia, with their capital at Ma'rib and the famous dam that sustained their agricultural economy. Their trade networks extended throughout the Arabian Peninsula and connected to the Mediterranean and Indian Ocean routes. While the kingdom declined by the 6th century CE, Sabaean tribal descendants dispersed throughout Arabia and are considered ancestors of many Qahtani tribes in the modern GCC states.

Based in the Yemeni highlands with their capital at Sana'a, they controlled important trade routes and agricultural regions. The dynasty was notable for the prominent role of Queen Arwa al-Sulayhi, who ruled effectively for several decades. Their influence extended into parts of the Arabian Peninsula but they are primarily significant to Yemeni rather than Gulf history, eventually being overthrown by the Ayyubids.

name: Sba'a

The confederation appears in early Arabic poetry and historical accounts as part of the complex tribal alliances of pre-Islamic and early Islamic Arabia. Like many ancient confederations, the specific composition and territories of Al-Sba'a are not well-documented in surviving historical sources. The name suggests a formal alliance structure based on the significant number seven in Arabian tribal tradition.

name: Shaiqiyah

description: The Shaiqiyah are a Sudanese Arab tribe historically based along the Nile River in northern Sudan, particularly in the Dongola region. They were known as skilled horsemen and warriors who controlled river trade routes and resisted Egyptian expansion in the early 19th century. The tribe has limited direct connection to Arabian Peninsula tribal politics but maintains cultural and genealogical ties to broader Arab tribal networks. Their alliance with the Manasir reflects historical connections between Sudanese and Gulf Arab tribal confederations.

name: Shammar
legitimacyNotes: A genuine tribal confederation with three main branches (Abdah, Al-Aslam, and Sinjarah) that evolved from the larger Tayy tribal group. The name change from Tayy to Shammar occurred by the 14th century when they became known in literature as Shammar.
ancestorStory: Descended from the Tayy tribe which migrated from Yemen to northern Arabian Peninsula. Led by Usma bin Luai, the Tayy invaded the mountains of Ajā and Salma from Banu Assad and Banu Tamim in northern Arabia in their exodus from Yemen in 115 CE.
traditionalEconomy: nomadic camel-herding, horse-breeding, city-dwelling in Ha'il, and agriculture in oases
description: The Shammar is a tribal Arab Qahtanite confederation descended from the Tayy, which migrated into northern Arabian Peninsula from Yemen in the second century. It is the largest branch of the Tayy and one of the largest and most influential Arab tribes. The historical seat of tribal leadership is in Ḥaʼil, and at its peak around the 1850s, the Shammar ruled much of central and northern Arabia from Riyadh to the frontiers of Syria and Upper Mesopotamia.

name: Sharqiyin

description: The Sharqiyin were long the dominant (traditionally Ghafiri) tribe along the East coast of the Trucial States and the second most numerous in the area around the start of the 19th century. They settled all along the East Coast from Kalba to Dibba, as well as in the Wadi Ham and Jiri plain. By the turn of the 20th century they were some 7,000 strong. After the Bani Yas, the Sharqiyin were the second most numerous tribe in the Trucial States. They were originally dependents of Sharjah but made several attempts to secede and declare independence, finally practically managing this from 1901 onwards, gaining British recognition as a Trucial State, Fujairah, in 1952.

They are essentially agricultural during winter months in mountain communities, migrating to coastal areas during warmer months for date season work and pearl fishing.

The tribe played a significant role in pre-Islamic poetry and warfare, with many members remaining Christian after the rise of Islam. name: Tayy

ancestorStory: According to early Arab genealogists, Julhumah ibn Udad was the progenitor of the Tayy. His surname 'Ṭayyiʾ' supposedly derived from the Arabic word 'ṭawā' meaning 'to plaster' because he was said to be the first to have plastered the walls of a well. His ancestry was traced to Kahlan ibn Saba ibn Ya'rub, great-grandson of Qahtan.
traditionalEconomy: pastoralism and control of trade routes
description: The Tayy are a large and ancient Arab tribe, among whose descendants today are the tribes of Bani Sakher and Shammar. Originally based in Yemen, they migrated to northern Arabia in the late 2nd century CE and settled in the Shammar and Salma Mountains, which became known as Jabal Tayy. They established relations with both the Sasanian and Byzantine empires, supplanted the Lakhmids as rulers of al-Hirah in the 610s, and played significant roles throughout Islamic history. The tribe was split during the Fasad War, with the Jadila branch converting to Christianity and migrating to Syria, while the Ghawth branch remained in Jabal Tayy.

subTribes.name: Shammar
subTribes.legitimacyNotes: A genuine tribal confederation with three main branches (Abdah, Al-Aslam, and Sinjarah) that evolved from the larger Tayy tribal group. The name change from Tayy to Shammar occurred by the 14th century when they became known in literature as Shammar.
subTribes.ancestorStory: Descended from the Tayy tribe which migrated from Yemen to northern Arabian Peninsula. Led by Usma bin Luai, the Tayy invaded the mountains of Ajā and Salma from Banu Assad and Banu Tamim in northern Arabia in their exodus from Yemen in 115 CE.

subTribes.description: The Shammar is a tribal Arab Qahtanite confederation descended from the Tayy, which migrated into northern Arabian Peninsula from Yemen in the second century. It is the largest branch of the Tayy and one of the largest and most influential Arab tribes. The historical seat of tribal leadership is in Ḥaʼil, and at its peak around the 1850s, the Shammar ruled much of central and northern Arabia from Riyadh to the frontiers of Syria and Upper Mesopotamia.

They were influential in tribal politics due to their ability to raise a force of 500 fighting men. The Bedouin Tunaij used Dhaid as their center with a fortified tower protecting their settlement. The Tunaij of Rams were primarily involved in pearl fishing, and during pearling season both Bedouin Tunaij and Shihuh would work as seasonal laborers on the coast.

The tribe played a significant role in the early consolidation of Saudi Arabia under Ibn Saud, initially as allies but later in rebellion during the Ikhwan movement. Today, members of the 'Ujman are settled throughout eastern Saudi Arabia and maintain their tribal identity while being integrated into modern Saudi society.

They played a significant role in early Islamic conquests and politics, notably through their alliance with the Umayyad dynasty. The tribe was particularly influential during the reign of Caliph Marwan ibn al-Hakam, whose wife was from Banu Kalb. While historically centered in the Levant, branches of the tribe spread throughout the Arabian Peninsula and some segments are found in modern Gulf states.

name: Banu Kinda
legitimacyNotes: Ancient Arab tribal confederation with royal lineage, held kingship over various Arab tribes in pre-Islamic era
ancestorStory: Descended from Kinda ibn Afsa ibn Adi ibn Haritha ibn Amr ibn Amir, establishing one of the most prominent pre-Islamic Arab tribal confederations. The tribe produced the famous Kindite kings who ruled over much of central and eastern Arabia.
description: Banu Kinda was one of the most powerful pre-Islamic Arab tribal confederations, originating from Yemen and later establishing kingdoms across central and eastern Arabia. They were known for their royal lineage and produced several kings who ruled over other Arab tribes in Najd and the eastern Arabian Peninsula during the 5th and 6th centuries CE. The confederation played a crucial role in pre-Islamic Arabian politics and warfare, with branches settling throughout the Arabian Peninsula. While the original confederation dissolved after the rise of Islam, various Kindite clans integrated into other tribal structures across the Gulf region.

Historical presence in eastern Arabian Peninsula

name: Banu Lakhm
legitimacyNotes: Ancient Arab tribal confederation with royal dynasty ruling the Lakhmid Kingdom
ancestorStory: Descended from Lakhm ibn Adi ibn Haritha ibn Amr ibn Amir, tracing back to the Azd. They established the powerful Lakhmid Kingdom in southern Iraq as vassals of the Sassanian Empire.
description: Banu Lakhm was a major pre-Islamic Arab tribal confederation that established the Lakhmid Kingdom in southern Iraq from the 3rd to 7th centuries CE. They served as vassals to the Sassanian Empire and controlled important trade routes between Arabia and Mesopotamia. The Lakhmids were known for their capital at Hira and played a crucial role in Arab-Persian relations before Islam. The confederation dissolved after the Islamic conquests, with descendants scattered across various regions including parts of the Arabian Peninsula.

relations.context: Both trace descent from Qahtanite origins in southern Iraq region

name: Banu Taghlib
legitimacyNotes: Ancient tribal lineage tracing to Adnanite origins, historically powerful in northern Arabia and Mesopotamia
ancestorStory: Taghlib ibn Wa'il was the eponymous ancestor of the tribe, part of the larger Bakr wa Taghlib confederation. The tribe became known for their fierce independence and rejection of tribute payments.
description: Banu Taghlib was a powerful pre-Islamic Arab tribe of Adnanite origin based in northern Arabia and Mesopotamia. They were known for their fierce independence, military prowess, and their famous refusal to pay tribute, preferring to pay double the jizya rather than zakat to maintain their autonomy. The tribe had a long-standing rivalry with their cousins Banu Bakr, leading to the famous Basus War that lasted for decades. While historically significant in Arabian tribal politics, they had limited direct influence in the modern Gulf states region.

relations.context: Descended from Adnanite lineage through Rabi'a ibn Nizar

name: Banu Uqayl
legitimacyNotes: Descended from Uqayl ibn Ka'b ibn Rabi'ah, established authority through military prowess and territorial control
ancestorStory: Uqayl was a prominent leader of the Rabi'ah tribal group in pre-Islamic Arabia. His descendants became known for their warrior traditions and established themselves across the Arabian Peninsula and Iraq.
description: Banu Uqayl was a major Bedouin tribe of Adnani lineage that dominated large parts of central and eastern Arabia, as well as Iraq, during the medieval period. They established the Uqaylid dynasty which ruled parts of Iraq and Syria from the 10th to 12th centuries. The tribe was known for its military capabilities and played significant roles in early Islamic conquests. Today, their descendants are scattered across the Arabian Peninsula, with some branches integrated into modern Gulf tribal structures.

name: Banu Murrah
legitimacyNotes: Traditional Bedouin tribal authority based on genealogical descent and customary leadership
ancestorStory: Descended from Murrah, a tribal progenitor traced through various Arabian genealogical lines. The tribe claims ancient Arabian heritage with roots in the central Arabian Peninsula.
description: Banu Murrah is a Bedouin tribe historically based in the central and eastern Arabian Peninsula, with presence extending into modern-day Saudi Arabia and the UAE. They were traditionally nomadic pastoralists who migrated seasonally across tribal territories. The tribe maintained traditional Bedouin social structures and played a role in regional tribal politics during the pre-modern period. Today, members of the tribe are settled citizens integrated into modern Gulf state societies while maintaining tribal identity.

name: Banu Zayd
legitimacyNotes: Lineage-based tribal authority through descent from Zayd
ancestorStory: Descended from Zayd, with multiple branches claiming this lineage across different regions. The name represents one of the most common tribal appellations in Arabian genealogy.
description: Banu Zayd represents one of several tribal groups claiming descent from an ancestor named Zayd, a common name in Arabian tribal genealogy. Various branches of this lineage have been documented across the Arabian Peninsula, with some settling in areas that later became part of modern GCC states. The tribe primarily engaged in traditional Bedouin pastoralism and followed seasonal migration patterns. Most Banu Zayd branches have been absorbed into larger tribal confederations or have become dormant as distinct political entities.

name: Al Sulaimi
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal authority based on genealogical descent and consensus
ancestorStory: Al Sulaimi trace their lineage to Sulaim ibn Mansour from the Qays Aylan branch. They are part of the broader Bani Sulaim confederation that spread across Arabia.
description: Al Sulaimi are a tribal branch descended from the larger Bani Sulaim confederation, traditionally based in western Arabia with some branches migrating to the Gulf region. They maintained nomadic pastoral traditions while some sections settled in oasis communities. The tribe has branches present in Saudi Arabia and other Gulf states, maintaining their tribal identity and customs. They remain active in contemporary Gulf society while preserving their genealogical connections to the broader Sulaimi lineage.
